text:
With the explosive growth of artificial intelligence, data centers are demanding increasingly higher computing power. 

Traditional copper cables can no longer meet the extreme high-speed transmission needs of massive data, and they also generate excessive heat. Therefore, "optical communication" (using light instead of electricity to transmit data) has become the key to solving the computing bottleneck.

In this sector, I believe three companies have the deepest "moats" (core competitive advantages) and the greatest future potential. 

Here is a simplified, easy-to-understand breakdown:

Lumentum (LITE): The "Profit Harvester" 

Mastering Core Physics
Lumentum's core advantage lies in its extremely high "technical barriers" and overwhelming pricing power. Simply put, as tech giants demand increasingly faster optical transmission speeds, it becomes incredibly difficult to manufacture lasers that can work stably at high temperatures while maintaining extremely pure light sources. Lumentum has successfully conquered this physics problem, producing top-tier 200G lasers. Because the technical threshold is so high—competitors either can't make them or can't make them well—Lumentum can sell its products at prices far above the industry average, causing its profit margins to soar. Furthermore, NVIDIA recently invested a massive $2 billion to lock in Lumentum's future production capacity. This means their current situation is one where demand far outstrips supply, and their only worry is manufacturing fast enough.

Coherent (COHR): The "Industry Tollbooth" 

Controlling Core Materials
If Lumentum wins on the extremely high profit margins of individual products, Coherent's moat is built on "absolute production scale" and "scarce manufacturing capabilities." Currently, the biggest bottleneck in the entire optical communication industry is a shortage of a critical material called Indium Phosphide (InP). Coherent is upgrading its production from traditional smaller wafers to larger 6-inch wafers. This upgrade allows them to produce four times as many devices at once while cutting the cost per device in half. What's most impressive is that they handle everything themselves, from the foundational materials to the final finished products, without relying on others. Also having received a $2 billion investment from NVIDIA, Coherent is transforming from a standard hardware supplier into a foundational "tollbooth" that all AI data center builds cannot bypass. While they are currently spending a lot of money to build out factories, in the long run, this scale advantage will be very difficult for competitors to overcome.

Marvell (MRVL): The "Chip Design Pioneer" 

Leading Next-Gen Architecture
Marvell's advantage lies in its powerful "ecosystem and chip design capabilities." Unlike the first two companies that build their own factories to manufacture hardware, Marvell focuses on the design of silicon photonics technology and custom networking chips (leaving the actual production to foundries). The future trend is that optical communication equipment will be increasingly and deeply integrated directly with computing chips, and Marvell stands right at the center of this technological architecture shift. They have already successfully shipped over 5 million complex photonic integrated circuits (PICs), proving their strong ability to execute and deliver technology. Marvell has the fiercest upward momentum among these three companies and is the purest play on the combination of custom chips and optical networks. However, it is important to note that precisely because everyone is so optimistic about it, its current stock price (valuation) is already very high, making it a choice where high risk and high reward coexist.

$LITE SUPPORT AT 600
$COHR SUPPORT AT 260
$MRVL SUPPORT AT 150

Traditional copper cables can no longer meet the extreme high-speed transmission needs of massive data, and they also generate excessive heat. Therefore, "optical communication" (using light instead of electricity to transmit data) has become the key to solving the computing bottleneck. In this sector, I believe three companies have the deepest "moats" (core competitive advantages) and the greatest future potential. Here is a simplified, easy-to-understand breakdown: Lumentum (LITE): The "Profit Harvester" Mastering Core Physics Lumentum's core advantage lies in its extremely high "technical barriers" and overwhelming pricing power. Simply put, as tech giants demand increasingly faster optical transmission speeds, it becomes incredibly difficult to manufacture lasers that can work stably at high temperatures while maintaining extremely pure light sources. Lumentum has successfully conquered this physics problem, producing top-tier 200G lasers. Because the technical threshold is so high—competitors either can't make them or can't make them well—Lumentum can sell its products at prices far above the industry average, causing its profit margins to soar. Furthermore, NVIDIA recently invested a massive $2 billion to lock in Lumentum's future production capacity. This means their current situation is one where demand far outstrips supply, and their only worry is manufacturing fast enough. Coherent (COHR): The "Industry Tollbooth" Controlling Core Materials If Lumentum wins on the extremely high profit margins of individual products, Coherent's moat is built on "absolute production scale" and "scarce manufacturing capabilities." Currently, the biggest bottleneck in the entire optical communication industry is a shortage of a critical material called Indium Phosphide (InP). Coherent is upgrading its production from traditional smaller wafers to larger 6-inch wafers. This upgrade allows them to produce four times as many devices at once while cutting the cost per device in half. What's most impressive is that they handle everything themselves, from the foundational materials to the final finished products, without relying on others. Also having received a $2 billion investment from NVIDIA, Coherent is transforming from a standard hardware supplier into a foundational "tollbooth" that all AI data center builds cannot bypass. While they are currently spending a lot of money to build out factories, in the long run, this scale advantage will be very difficult for competitors to overcome. Marvell (MRVL): The "Chip Design Pioneer" Leading Next-Gen Architecture Marvell's advantage lies in its powerful "ecosystem and chip design capabilities." Unlike the first two companies that build their own factories to manufacture hardware, Marvell focuses on the design of silicon photonics technology and custom networking chips (leaving the actual production to foundries). The future trend is that optical communication equipment will be increasingly and deeply integrated directly with computing chips, and Marvell stands right at the center of this technological architecture shift. They have already successfully shipped over 5 million complex photonic integrated circuits (PICs), proving their strong ability to execute and deliver technology. Marvell has the fiercest upward momentum among these three companies and is the purest play on the combination of custom chips and optical networks. However, it is important to note that precisely because everyone is so optimistic about it, its current stock price (valuation) is already very high, making it a choice where high risk and high reward coexist. text:
Where the RS100 leader group stands right now. Logging the day today's semi collapse started firing sharp-drop flags across the core holdings.

They still hold RS100, but the day-change and 200MA distance show a clear split.

■"Sharp-drop watch" lit
・VICR (electronic components) RS100, −7.3% d/d, falling, 200MA distance +44.3%
・MU (memory/HBM) RS100, −4.3% d/d, soft, distance +100.1%
Tagged sharp-drop watch. A large 200MA distance = more room to unwind from an overextended move.

■Deep pullback / falling
・ICHR (semi equip) RS100, −9.9% d/d, deep pullback, −19.5% off 52wk high
・EVC (radio/TV) RS100, −5.3% d/d, deep pullback, falling
・SNDK (data storage) RS100, −12.6% d/d, broken, −28.3% off 52wk high
SNDK led the drop today at −12.6% — a 7/2-entry name breaking down within 8 sessions.

■"Momentum thinning" (defensive lean)
MXL, MU, VICR, ICHR, SNDK, WDC all carry the thinning tag — consistent with the run-thinning phase.

■Dropped out of leadership
・AXTI: RS100 but ▼61 = out of leadership (RS<85, falling), −64.2% off 52wk high
・LITE: out of leadership, AI optical/photonics
The optical names still show an RS figure but have effectively fallen out of leadership.

■Exception: ATEX (wireless carrier)
The only green. RS100, +2.4% d/d, 200MA distance +163.5% — but tagged "extended, don't chase." Too stretched to add new.

—
Read (my view):
This morning I flagged F3 queue-breakdown entering alert and said prioritize exit management — this image is the substance of that. Even across the RS100 core, −4% to −13% daily drops spread through semis, memory, and optical. Names with 200MA distance above +100% (MU, EVC, ICHR, MXL, ATEX) have strong trends but carry room to give back an overextended move.

Meanwhile the smaller-distance names — VICR (+44%) and SNDK (+122% but −28% off its 52wk high) — have already been cut hard, so remaining downside may be more limited.

For holdings management, I read the priority exit candidates as names where (1) a "sharp-drop / falling" tag and (2) a large 200MA distance overlap. New entries: mostly pass in this sharp-drop phase, wait for a bounce to confirm. *For the record — not investment advice.*

$SNDK $AXTI $MXL $EVC $VICR $MU $LITE $ATEX $ICHR $WDC

They drop 45 days after the quarter ends. By the time you see what Citadel bought, the trade is "done." That is the wrong way to read them. A 13F is a hedge fund's homework. Every fund managing over $100M has to file one, every quarter, 45 days late. Think of it like the test scores a teacher posts in May. The exam was back in March, but the patterns still tell you who studied. The trick is not the filing. It is the OVERLAP. We watch 10 funds: Citadel, Two Sigma, Renaissance, DE Shaw, Millennium, Point72, Coatue, Tiger, Lone Pine, Maverick. Some are quants. Some are stock pickers. Some are macro. They almost never agree. When 4 or more of those 10 add the SAME obscure mid-cap in the SAME quarter, we call it a cluster. Clusters are not random. Why it matters: Institutional buying shows up 30 to 90 days before earnings momentum kicks in. The funds see the order book. We see the buyers. The press release comes last. Real example. Power transformers ( $GEV $ETN $HUBB $PWR ) lit up as a cluster in Q1. The "AI power bottleneck" thesis was on the desk before retail had a name for it. Lead times now sit at 130 weeks. Hyperscalers are paying anything to skip the line. What is clustering now: 1. Silicon photonics ( $LITE $COHR $AAOI ): replacing copper wires with light inside AI servers, because copper cannot move data fast enough at the rack level. $LITE is the only supplier shipping the high-speed laser parts at the volume next-gen AI optics need. 2. Custom AI ASICs ( $AVGO $MRVL $ALAB $CRDO ): the chips hyperscalers design in-house instead of buying off the shelf from $NVDA. Multi-strategy funds stacked these names through Q1. 3. Uranium and $SMR ( $CCJ $UEC $NXE ): not a tourist trade anymore. Hyperscalers need clean 24/7 power. Small modular reactors are the only design that can sit next to a data center. How we read it on the desk: One fund buying = noise. Two funds buying = coincidence. Four or more in the same quarter = a thesis somebody is willing to bet billions on, before the catalyst hits the tape. That is why we read the "stale" filings before earnings. The earnings number is the proof. text:
TLDR of Innolight investor relations takeaways:

1. "Overall, 1.6T market demand has not contracted; instead, 800G demand has increased significantly compared with previous expectations"

Prob most important takeaway as a whole was 800G demand revision (also longer tail demand). Which is a bullish read through on US transceiver makers like $AAOI, $COHR, $LITE. 

Lot of new customers like neoclouds, AI model companies, contributing to overall demand rather than just hyperscalers, diversification always a bonus. 

2. Innolight said the shortage covers the module supply chain broadly including:

- Optical chips.
- Electrical chips.
- PCBs.
- Other module materials

From last ER, I think they singled out EMLs and CW optical chips as the most constrained. So Innolight's bottleneck list mention broadened since then. 

They expect some of the component availability to improve gradually from the second half of 2026 through the first half of 2027. 

Think a lot of this is already known from earlier though. 

But just some confirmation + easing timelines (EML is extremely bottlenecked, same with CW, this is probably talking about other components). 

3.  Innolight said module-production equipment is not the constraint. Equipment lead times remain relatively short. 

So this isn't really a bottleneck compared to others. 

4. The overall proportion of silicon photonics continues to trend upward. Last year it was mainly 800G. This year, some 800G customers are further increasing their silicon-photonics proportion. 1.6T also added some new customers

Positive for SiPH penetration eg. $SIVE / $JBL, since this shifts away from EML toward CW. 

Basically: main surprising takeaway is just 800g demand go brrr. 

Apart from that just reaffirming bottlenecks/timelines/market speculation.

text:
CPI tomorrow 

The inflation report that markets treat like a Rorschach test: one side sees cooling, the other sees the Fed’s worst nightmare reaccelerating. Shelter still lagging, goods deflation throwing in the towel, core services whispering “not so fast.”

Hot print = real yields spike, cuts repriced. 
Cool print = everyone breathes, then wonders why they panicked.

Disinflation’s victory lap just got interrupted. Structural bottleneck plays (AI infra, photonics, etc.) don’t care about the noise — they own the signal.

Trade the reaction, not the headline. Or as we call it: adulting in macro.

Skew thoughts?

text:
$CRDO's board published its own price target in an SEC filing last month. Almost nobody read it.

Buried in a June 8-K: a one-time CEO award. 1.44M shares that only vest as revenue climbs from $2.5B to $7.5B and the stock clears hurdles from $244 to $489 by 2031.

The CEO gets nothing unless shareholders triple first.

Boards don't structure comp like that for companies they expect to grind.

Consider who built this. The CTO spent 11 years leading analog design for $MRVL's storage chips, recovering impossibly faint signals billions of times per second without errors. Same physics as the engine inside every Credo cable. He didn't pivot into a hot market. He transplanted a mastered craft. His stake: $1.3 billion.

The seed check came from Marvell's own co-founder. $3M, now worth about $1 billion. Lip-Bu Tan, now running $INTC, led the round.

Founded, funded, and governed by Marvell royalty. Today, Marvell is chasing them with a copycat cable program. The students became the masters.

The smart money kept coming. To win $AMZN in 2021, Credo granted it a warrant on 4M shares at $10.74. Fully vested, now worth ~$1B. Amazon didn't just pick its cable supplier. It took a billion-dollar equity stake in the outcome.

And the setup nobody connected: the optical supply chain is short of lasers through 2027, bottlenecked by a substrate under Chinese export controls. $CRDO  just spent $1.3B on a photonics architecture that needs far fewer lasers, and its FY28 cable line replaces them entirely with microLEDs made outside the bottleneck. In a two-year famine, the vendor selling the workaround collects scarcity rent.

The famine is documented everywhere. The connection is not. The connection is the trade.

$NVDA is the one logo missing from the customer list, and that's exactly why the Street can't model this company.

Revenue tripled. Guided +80% again. 68% gross margins, zero debt, half of revenue becoming profit.

Full deep dive out now! 👇

text:
More than $15 BILLION has moved into silicon photonics since late 2025.

Look at what the industry is actually doing:

• $NVDA committed $4B to $COHR and $LITE
• $MRVL acquired Celestial AI for $3.25B
• Ayar Labs raised $500M
• $AMD, $GFS, $CIEN and $CRDO all acquired optical technology

This is no longer normal sector investment.

It is a land grab for control of the AI interconnect stack.

The biggest winners may not just be the companies selling complete transceivers.

They could be the least replaceable layers underneath them:

• Lasers
• InP substrates
• Photonic foundries
• Advanced packaging
• Testing equipment

That is why I’m researching:

$SIVE $AXTI $TSEM $AEHR

AI compute scaled first.

Now the entire industry is fighting to control what connects it.

text:
some thoughts and what I’m doing:

- TSMC earnings: highly bullish. two things caught my eye:

> massive capex, significant raise for 2026 and a further $100B in U.S. equipment players will benefit. great buys $AMAT $ASML $LRCX $KLAC 

> quoting the CEO: AI demand is much stronger than what we said before 

- $TSEM committing billions to silicon photonics and optical packaging. optical stack benefits such as $LITE $AAOI 

- on the other side of the table, $GOOG internal concerns over losing to OpenAI and Anthropic. rate cut odds are much higher than capex cuts imo

- Kimi K3 benchmark news, I read mixed reviews and I’m not an expert but probably overhyped like everything else with China. however, if this spooks enough people we might get a capitulation candle. 

- U.S. lawmakers asking Trump to ban Chinese memory

> simply put, fundamentals are not just good, they’re improving while the market is crashing. so basically fundamentals vs. positioning, I choose fundamentals. 

- on my trading account, I’ve already bought heavily during June lows. took some profit but I’m down -35% from ATHs. then I’ve transferred funds as I said and added significantly to my existing positions on July 7-8. 

- as of writing, now I’m down -3% on my portfolio. if this breakdowns on the charts are bear traps (tbh I’m favoring this), nothing to worry. if not and we get another material crash, I’ll once again add heavily. $SOXX chart pattern target is around 450s which is $700 for $MU and $1070 for $SNDK, give or take. however, I highly doubt that with earnings season starting next week with hyperscalers and $INTC 

> I think this is a bear trap. highly bullish developments from the sector and too obvious patterns on charts of the most crowded trades. just like the recent DXY breakout. we’ll see.

text:
There have been signs for months that the optics/photonics industry players were cognizant of InP substrate supply constraints (both resource and politically-driven) and started developing solutions around them, including GaA based VCSELs

Reason being that VCSEL supply chain is mature, well-supported and proven with massive deployment (through consumer electronic devices). 3D sensing VCSEL has existed for almost as long as the cameras on our smartphones

That's why I was confused when people dissed the slow and wide approach (shall not name who but there were a few prominent ones on X)

The biggest signal was when OCI MSA was formed earlier in March this year. The premise was:

Gen1: 4λ x 50Gbps NRZ (200Gbps/direction) 

GEN2 400Gbps/direction BiDi for up to 800Gbps per fiber

Roadmap includes scaling wavelength counts and data rates to reach 3.2Tbps per fiber and beyond (i.e. 1.6Tbps/direction BiDi) in interoperable form factors supporting pluggable, NPO/OBO and CPO

But that's not to say InP is dead. On the contrary, they are critical for long-range optics transmission

In fact, some companies like AXTI produces both InP and GaA substrates. But look into the risk factors for each company, especially the geopolitical ones because these are incredibly difficult to navigate

Lastly, on microLED. I don't believe in them because you need lots of them and fibers to work. Alignment is going to be tricky (amongst other issues). Not a scalable solution

text:
Buzzberg Daily Alpha.

We scanned every video, Substack article, tracked X research post, and Reddit discussion in Buzzberg’s feed.

The clearest conclusion: this was primarily a positioning and expectations reset in semiconductors, Korean memory, and high-beta AI, not evidence that end demand has collapsed.

Why the market fell

Crowding was the first problem. A widely shared factor snapshot showed Goldman’s high-beta momentum basket down 24% MTD, while Morgan Stanley’s tech-momentum 17-day rate of change fell 35%, its worst reading in 27 years. Multiple speakers described leverage being unwound across names already 30–50% below their highs.

Korea amplified the move. Samsung and SK Hynix had accumulated heavy retail margin exposure, while leveraged single-stock ETFs created additional daily rebalancing flows. The SK Hynix ADR also traded at a large premium while conversion constraints prevented normal arbitrage, separating $SKHY from the Korean listing. Regulatory intervention and thin holiday liquidity added further pressure.

CXMT became the narrative catalyst. Its approaching IPO revived concerns that Chinese DRAM capacity could eventually compress commodity-memory margins. Bulls argue that CXMT remains years behind in advanced DRAM and HBM; bears are looking beyond the current shortage and pricing the eventual increase in supply.

The more important signal was that good news stopped working. $TSM reported roughly 78% profit growth, raised capex to $60–64B, and guided above consensus, yet the report failed to lift semiconductors. The market is no longer questioning current AI demand. It is questioning how much future growth was already priced into the equities.

A harder, still-unconfirmed bear thesis emerged on Reddit: $AMZN selling Trainium racks, Meta selling compute, and weakness in IBM’s infrastructure business could indicate spare AI capacity. The bullish interpretation is that hyperscalers are simply monetizing constrained infrastructure rather than reducing their own deployments.

Power and permitting are another emerging constraint. New York paused large data-center projects unless developers provide their own power. That does not necessarily destroy AI capex, but it could redirect spending from chips toward onsite generation, grid equipment, and less restrictive states.

Bull case

The fundamental data remain supportive. TSMC is still unable to satisfy all leading-edge demand, ASML raised its outlook and production capacity, and memory specialists continue to expect HBM demand to outrun supply through 2028.

The less obvious signal came from CoreWeave exploring memory puts. Jukan’s interpretation is that this may confirm memory LTAs are becoming more binding: customers must hedge contracted prices instead of cancelling volumes when spot prices fall.
If hyperscalers maintain or raise capex guidance, the current decline remains a positioning reset inside an intact AI investment cycle.

Bear case

Demand can remain strong while stocks continue falling. The internet kept growing through the dot-com crash; what broke was the price investors had paid for that growth.

TSMC’s negative price reaction suggests that beats are already expected. CXMT creates a longer-term margin risk, ASML’s proposed price increases shift costs toward foundries, and data-center power constraints could delay deployments. Mary Ann Bartels sees the potential for a 30–50% semiconductor correction before the uptrend resumes.

Mark Cudmore also expects additional weakness in Korea and global chips based on poor technicals. He did not disclose a short, however, and remains positive on the AI-capex trade until hyperscalers begin cutting expenditure. In his framework, that would be the fundamental “game over” signal.
Who is actually buying

@labubu_trader  disclosed a new $SNDK  purchase while holding $MU, $SKHY/000660.KS, and $NBIS , with MAG7 still his largest exposure.

@michaelsikand  explicitly put $100K to work across six baskets, including memory ($MU/$SNDK/$EWY), photonics ($AAOI/$MRVL), AI factories ($NBIS), space ($RKLB/$ASTS), and defense.

Who is cautious

Jeong Jae-ho believes the Korean bull market remains intact, but says recent Samsung/SK Hynix buyers should reduce exposure on rebounds. Low-basis holders may keep core positions; his preferred rotation is toward Korean banks and gradual NAVER accumulation.

Mary Ann Bartels prefers Mag 7, particularly $GOOGL , and believes $META may have bottomed. She is waiting for a deeper semiconductor correction before buying that sector.

The feed’s message is clear: fundamentals have not broken, but the market has shifted from rewarding strong results to selling them. The next decisive test is hyperscaler guidance. Maintained capex supports the positioning-unwind thesis; capex cuts would turn it into a fundamental downturn.

text:
ST마이크로 실리콘 포토닉스, 300mm는 "개발 중"이 아니라 "양산 중"이다

AI 클러스터가 랙 단위에서 랙 '군집' 단위로 커지면서, 컴퓨팅 자체만큼 데이터 이동(광학 인터커넥트)이 중요해지고 있다. ST마이크로일렉트로닉스는 이미 확립한 300mm 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼(PIC100)의 산업화 — 즉 패키징·전기적 통합 역량 확보 — 를 다음 승부처로 보고 있다. 여기서 짚어야 할 건 "300mm로 가는 중"이라는 프레이밍이 아니라, "이미 300mm에서 만들고 있고, AI 수요가 그 양산 볼륨을 밀어붙이고 있다"는 사실 관계다.

📌 1. 핵심 기술 분석
구리의 한계, 광학으로 전환
짧은 거리 전기 배선용으로는 여전히 구리가 쓰이지만, 대역폭이 커지고 클러스터가 확장되면서 구리는 도달거리·전력효율·신호무결성 세 가지 측면에서 한계에 부딪히고 있다. ST 옵티컬·RF 파운드리 사업부 총괄 Sylvie Gellida는 "AI 데이터센터는 랙 내 더 많은 라우팅 능력과, 더 좁은 물리적 공간 안에서 더 큰 대역폭을 필요로 한다"고 밝혔다.
PIC100, 이미 300mm 양산 중
ST의 현재 실리콘 포토닉스 기술인 PIC100은 100-GBaud 신호로 lane당 200G를 지원한다. 핵심은 여기다 — Gellida는 "우리의 차별점은 200G/lane을 지원한다는 것 자체가 아니라, 이 기술을 300mm 웨이퍼에서 CMOS급 수율로 양산하고 있다는 것"이라고 강조했다. 실제 발언: "Today we're supplying silicon photonics wafers in Crolles, France, on 300-mm wafers." 즉 이건 "300mm로의 전환"이 아니라 "이미 검증된 300mm 플랫폼의 볼륨 확장" 스토리다.

Sicoya의 1.6T 증명
베를린 소재 Sicoya는 PIC100 기반 OSFP 모듈로 1.6T DR8(8-lane × 200G/lane) 동작을 시연했다. TIA(트랜스임피던스 증폭기)를 PIC 위에 다이 스태킹하는 방식으로, 모놀리식 통합이 아니다. Sicoya CTO Hanjo Rhee는 "다이 스태킹이 더 나은 선택"이라며, 파운드리 확장성과 기술 업데이트 속도 측면에서 유리하다고 밝혔다. 단, 이 시연은 여전히 플러거블(pluggable) 광트랜시버 영역이며 CPO(공동 패키지 광학)가 아니다.
패키징 로드맵 — TSV는 아직 개발 단계
ST는 PIC100과 BiCMOS 플랫폼용 TSV(실리콘 관통전극) 옵션을 개발 중이다. 이는 광학 소자를 프로세서/스위치 ASIC에 더 가깝게 붙이기 위한 후속 단계로, 현재 양산되는 기술이 아니라 로드맵상 개발 항목이라는 점을 구분해야 한다.

📌 2. 밸류체인
ST의 고객 구조 — 하이퍼스케일러 직접 공급 아님
ST의 직접 고객은 하이퍼스케일러 자체가 아니라 트랜시버 모듈 업체와 PIC 설계 하우스다. 이들이 ST의 실리콘 포토닉스·BiCMOS 기술로 광엔진·트랜시버·모듈을 만들고, 이것이 다시 하이퍼스케일러 생태계로 공급되는 구조다. ST는 BiCMOS(레이저 드라이버·전자 IC), STM32(모듈 제어), 패키징·테스트까지 아우르는 "원스톱 숍" 전략을 추구한다.
시장 단계 구분 — 플러거블 → NPO → CPO
Gellida에 따르면 근시일 최대 시장은 여전히 플러거블 광트랜시버(특히 scale-out 네트워크용)이며, NPO(광학을 ASIC에 더 가깝게 붙이는 중간 단계)가 다음이고, CPO는 신뢰성·서비스 가능성 이슈가 해결된 이후 더 나중에 온다. 이 순서를 생략하고 "CPO 임박"으로 서술하면 시점 오독을 유발한다.

경쟁 파운드리 구도
Sicoya CTO에 따르면 Tower Semiconductor는 상당한 포토닉스 캐파를 갖고 있지만 대부분 200mm 기반이며 이미 예약이 꽉 찬 상태다. TSMC도 포토닉스 라인업이 있지만 접근성 이슈가 있다. 300mm 기반 ST 플랫폼이 Sicoya 같은 업체에 "볼륨 확장 가능한 파운드리 파트너"로서 대안이 된다는 것이 이 기사의 경쟁 구도 핵심이다.

📌 3. 시장 변수 및 향후 관전 포인트
NPO/CPO로의 전환 속도: 서비스 모델(광커넥터 탈부착 등 조립 공정)이 아직 대량 양산 수준으로 해결되지 않음 — 이 부분 진척이 관전 포인트

ST의 300mm 캐파 확장 속도와 TSV 등 차세대 패키징 옵션의 양산 진입 시점
Marvell의 Celestial AI(2월, 35억 달러), Polariton Technologies 인수 등 광인터커넥트 관련 M&A 동향과 ST·Sicoya 생태계와의 경쟁/보완 관계

📌 4. 종합 시사점
본질은 "새로운 광학 기술의 등장"이 아니라 "이미 검증된 300mm 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼의 산업화 경쟁"이다. 기술 성능(200G/lane) 자체는 경쟁사도 도달 가능한 수준이라는 게 ST 스스로의 설명이고, 진짜 차별점은 대량 양산 캐파(300mm)와 패키징 통합 역량이라는 점이다. 이는 투자 관점에서 "누가 더 빠른 광학 소자를 만드느냐"보다 "누가 더 빨리, 더 안정적으로 대량 생산·패키징 체계를 갖추느냐"의 경쟁으로 봐야 한다는 뜻이다.

📌 5. 글로벌 핵심 종목과 투자 인사이트
직접 관련
STMicroelectronics ($STM , NYSE) — PIC100/BiCMOS 300mm 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼 공급사, 크롤레(프랑스) 팹에서 양산

Sicoya — 비상장(독일), PIC100 기반 1.6T 트랜시버 시연 파트너

경쟁/비교 대상 (비상장 또는 별도 사업부 소속으로 직접 티커 연결 어려움)

Tower Semiconductor — 200mm 중심 포토닉스 파운드리, 캐파 포화 상태

TSMC — 포토닉스 라인업 보유하나 접근성 이슈로 언급됨

국내 상장 종목 중 이번 기사에서 직접 언급되거나 확인 가능한 밸류체인 연결고리는 없다. 국내 광통신 부품·CPO 관련주(예: 광트랜시버 모듈 업체 등)와의 연관성은 이 기사만으로는 근거가 부족하므로 별도 검증 없이 엮지 않는다.

※ 본 내용은 정보 제공 목적이며, 투자 판단 및 책임은 독자 본인에게 있습니다.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📖 용어 설명
PIC (Photonic Integrated Circuit): 광신호를 생성·변조·검출하는 회로 요소를 하나의 칩에 집적한 광집적회로
GBaud / lane: 광 신호의 심볼 전송 속도(100-GBaud) 및 한 개의 물리적 전송 경로(lane)당 데이터 속도(200G)를 뜻함. 8개 lane을 묶으면 1.6T(테라비트)가 됨
다이 스태킹 (Die Stacking) vs 모놀리식 통합: 다이 스태킹은 광소자(PIC)와 전자소자(TIA 등)를 별도로 제작한 뒤 물리적으로 쌓아 연결하는 방식. 모놀리식 통합은 애초에 하나의 칩 위에 광·전자 소자를 함께 만드는 방식으로, 신호 경로는 짧지만 개발·파운드리 유연성이 떨어짐

TSV (Through-Silicon Via, 실리콘 관통전극): 칩을 수직으로 관통해 여러 층의 칩을 전기적으로 연결하는 배선 기술. 연결 손실을 줄이고 집적도를 높이는 데 쓰임

플러거블(Pluggable) 광트랜시버: 스위치·서버 외부 포트에 꽂아 쓰는 착탈식 광통신 모듈. 현재 데이터센터 광인터커넥트의 주력 형태

NPO (Near-Packaged Optics): 광학 엔진을 ASIC/GPU 패키지에 더 가깝게(같은 기판 위 등) 배치하는 중간 단계 접근

CPO (Co-Packaged Optics, 공동 패키지 광학): 광학 엔진을 ASIC/GPU와 동일 패키지 안에 통합하는 방식. 신호 손실은 최소화되지만 신뢰성·서비스(교체·수리) 문제 해결이 아직 진행 중

ST마이크로일렉트로닉스는 이미 확립한 300mm 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼(PIC100)의 산업화 — 즉 https://t.co/1QGphKsntQ", "id_str": "2077885355785896335", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "ko", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 2, "retweet_count": 1, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "2062077810836123649"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzc4ODUzNTU0NDYxMDgxNjE=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": [{"from_index": 0, "richtext_types": ["Bold", "Italic"], "to_index": 44}, {"from_index": 308, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 319}, {"from_index": 1326, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 1333}, {"from_index": 1988, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2011}, {"from_index": 2253, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2262}, {"from_index": 2520, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2541}, {"from_index": 3008, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 3013}]}, "text": "ST마이크로 실리콘 포토닉스, 300mm는 "개발 중"이 아니라 "양산 중"이다 AI 클러스터가 랙 단위에서 랙 '군집' 단위로 커지면서, 컴퓨팅 자체만큼 데이터 이동(광학 인터커넥트)이 중요해지고 있다. ST마이크로일렉트로닉스는 이미 확립한 300mm 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼(PIC100)의 산업화 — 즉 패키징·전기적 통합 역량 확보 — 를 다음 승부처로 보고 있다. 여기서 짚어야 할 건 "300mm로 가는 중"이라는 프레이밍이 아니라, "이미 300mm에서 만들고 있고, AI 수요가 그 양산 볼륨을 밀어붙이고 있다"는 사실 관계다. 📌 1. 핵심 기술 분석 구리의 한계, 광학으로 전환 짧은 거리 전기 배선용으로는 여전히 구리가 쓰이지만, 대역폭이 커지고 클러스터가 확장되면서 구리는 도달거리·전력효율·신호무결성 세 가지 측면에서 한계에 부딪히고 있다. ST 옵티컬·RF 파운드리 사업부 총괄 Sylvie Gellida는 "AI 데이터센터는 랙 내 더 많은 라우팅 능력과, 더 좁은 물리적 공간 안에서 더 큰 대역폭을 필요로 한다"고 밝혔다. PIC100, 이미 300mm 양산 중 ST의 현재 실리콘 포토닉스 기술인 PIC100은 100-GBaud 신호로 lane당 200G를 지원한다. 핵심은 여기다 — Gellida는 "우리의 차별점은 200G/lane을 지원한다는 것 자체가 아니라, 이 기술을 300mm 웨이퍼에서 CMOS급 수율로 양산하고 있다는 것"이라고 강조했다. 실제 발언: "Today we're supplying silicon photonics wafers in Crolles, France, on 300-mm wafers." 즉 이건 "300mm로의 전환"이 아니라 "이미 검증된 300mm 플랫폼의 볼륨 확장" 스토리다. Sicoya의 1.6T 증명 베를린 소재 Sicoya는 PIC100 기반 OSFP 모듈로 1.6T DR8(8-lane × 200G/lane) 동작을 시연했다. TIA(트랜스임피던스 증폭기)를 PIC 위에 다이 스태킹하는 방식으로, 모놀리식 통합이 아니다. Sicoya CTO Hanjo Rhee는 "다이 스태킹이 더 나은 선택"이라며, 파운드리 확장성과 기술 업데이트 속도 측면에서 유리하다고 밝혔다. 단, 이 시연은 여전히 플러거블(pluggable) 광트랜시버 영역이며 CPO(공동 패키지 광학)가 아니다. 패키징 로드맵 — TSV는 아직 개발 단계 ST는 PIC100과 BiCMOS 플랫폼용 TSV(실리콘 관통전극) 옵션을 개발 중이다. 이는 광학 소자를 프로세서/스위치 ASIC에 더 가깝게 붙이기 위한 후속 단계로, 현재 양산되는 기술이 아니라 로드맵상 개발 항목이라는 점을 구분해야 한다. 📌 2. 밸류체인 ST의 고객 구조 — 하이퍼스케일러 직접 공급 아님 ST의 직접 고객은 하이퍼스케일러 자체가 아니라 트랜시버 모듈 업체와 PIC 설계 하우스다. 이들이 ST의 실리콘 포토닉스·BiCMOS 기술로 광엔진·트랜시버·모듈을 만들고, 이것이 다시 하이퍼스케일러 생태계로 공급되는 구조다. ST는 BiCMOS(레이저 드라이버·전자 IC), STM32(모듈 제어), 패키징·테스트까지 아우르는 "원스톱 숍" 전략을 추구한다. 시장 단계 구분 — 플러거블 → NPO → CPO Gellida에 따르면 근시일 최대 시장은 여전히 플러거블 광트랜시버(특히 scale-out 네트워크용)이며, NPO(광학을 ASIC에 더 가깝게 붙이는 중간 단계)가 다음이고, CPO는 신뢰성·서비스 가능성 이슈가 해결된 이후 더 나중에 온다. 이 순서를 생략하고 "CPO 임박"으로 서술하면 시점 오독을 유발한다. 경쟁 파운드리 구도 Sicoya CTO에 따르면 Tower Semiconductor는 상당한 포토닉스 캐파를 갖고 있지만 대부분 200mm 기반이며 이미 예약이 꽉 찬 상태다. TSMC도 포토닉스 라인업이 있지만 접근성 이슈가 있다. 300mm 기반 ST 플랫폼이 Sicoya 같은 업체에 "볼륨 확장 가능한 파운드리 파트너"로서 대안이 된다는 것이 이 기사의 경쟁 구도 핵심이다. 📌 3. 시장 변수 및 향후 관전 포인트 NPO/CPO로의 전환 속도: 서비스 모델(광커넥터 탈부착 등 조립 공정)이 아직 대량 양산 수준으로 해결되지 않음 — 이 부분 진척이 관전 포인트 ST의 300mm 캐파 확장 속도와 TSV 등 차세대 패키징 옵션의 양산 진입 시점 Marvell의 Celestial AI(2월, 35억 달러), Polariton Technologies 인수 등 광인터커넥트 관련 M&A 동향과 ST·Sicoya 생태계와의 경쟁/보완 관계 📌 4. 종합 시사점 본질은 "새로운 광학 기술의 등장"이 아니라 "이미 검증된 300mm 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼의 산업화 경쟁"이다. 기술 성능(200G/lane) 자체는 경쟁사도 도달 가능한 수준이라는 게 ST 스스로의 설명이고, 진짜 차별점은 대량 양산 캐파(300mm)와 패키징 통합 역량이라는 점이다. 이는 투자 관점에서 "누가 더 빠른 광학 소자를 만드느냐"보다 "누가 더 빨리, 더 안정적으로 대량 생산·패키징 체계를 갖추느냐"의 경쟁으로 봐야 한다는 뜻이다. 📌 5. 글로벌 핵심 종목과 투자 인사이트 직접 관련 STMicroelectronics ($STM , NYSE) — PIC100/BiCMOS 300mm 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼 공급사, 크롤레(프랑스) 팹에서 양산 Sicoya — 비상장(독일), PIC100 기반 1.6T 트랜시버 시연 파트너 경쟁/비교 대상 (비상장 또는 별도 사업부 소속으로 직접 티커 연결 어려움) Tower Semiconductor — 200mm 중심 포토닉스 파운드리, 캐파 포화 상태 TSMC — 포토닉스 라인업 보유하나 접근성 이슈로 언급됨 국내 상장 종목 중 이번 기사에서 직접 언급되거나 확인 가능한 밸류체인 연결고리는 없다. 국내 광통신 부품·CPO 관련주(예: 광트랜시버 모듈 업체 등)와의 연관성은 이 기사만으로는 근거가 부족하므로 별도 검증 없이 엮지 않는다. ※ 본 내용은 정보 제공 목적이며, 투자 판단 및 책임은 독자 본인에게 있습니다. ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 📖 용어 설명 PIC (Photonic Integrated Circuit): 광신호를 생성·변조·검출하는 회로 요소를 하나의 칩에 집적한 광집적회로 GBaud / lane: 광 신호의 심볼 전송 속도(100-GBaud) 및 한 개의 물리적 전송 경로(lane)당 데이터 속도(200G)를 뜻함. 8개 lane을 묶으면 1.6T(테라비트)가 됨 다이 스태킹 (Die Stacking) vs 모놀리식 통합: 다이 스태킹은 광소자(PIC)와 전자소자(TIA 등)를 별도로 제작한 뒤 물리적으로 쌓아 연결하는 방식. 모놀리식 통합은 애초에 하나의 칩 위에 광·전자 소자를 함께 만드는 방식으로, 신호 경로는 짧지만 개발·파운드리 유연성이 떨어짐 TSV (Through-Silicon Via, 실리콘 관통전극): 칩을 수직으로 관통해 여러 층의 칩을 전기적으로 연결하는 배선 기술. 연결 손실을 줄이고 집적도를 높이는 데 쓰임 플러거블(Pluggable) 광트랜시버: 스위치·서버 외부 포트에 꽂아 쓰는 착탈식 광통신 모듈. 현재 데이터센터 광인터커넥트의 주력 형태 NPO (Near-Packaged Optics): 광학 엔진을 ASIC/GPU 패키지에 더 가깝게(같은 기판 위 등) 배치하는 중간 단계 접근 CPO (Co-Packaged Optics, 공동 패키지 광학): 광학 엔진을 ASIC/GPU와 동일 패키지 안에 통합하는 방식. 신호 손실은 최소화되지만 신뢰성·서비스(교체·수리) 문제 해결이 아직 진행 중"}}}, "rest_id": "2077885355785896335", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "613", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Almost every assumption for data centers is based upon current compute rate, heat, and energy use

Little thought is given to #photonics based computing which is 1,000 times more efficient - and is only 5 to 10 years away at scale

It feels like a Ponzi scheme where first the money goes to AI, then data centers, then orbital data centers

By the time we get orbital data centers in place in space, the cost equation will make it easier to have them on Earth, the moon or in space - whatever is most convenient without regard to location 

You'll need less environmental permitting for a future data center than you would for a refrigerator

I understand that Starship for orbital data centers is a great cash flow consideration for SpaceX 

It would certainly create the transport demand and capital needed for SpaceX to fund a Mars colony

I think we need to look behind the curtain to see what's going on 

🚀💫

text:
If a Chinese company like Moonshot can produce a frontier-level model despite restricted access to Nvidia's most advanced GPUs, investors may ask

Do future AI models really require as many top-end Nvidia chips as previously assumed?

Can software efficiency offset some of the need for massive compute?

If the answer is YES, then the market may lower its expectations for future GPU demand growth. That can hurt Nvidia and companies tied to AI infrastructure (HBM, networking, optics, photonics, testing, etc.).

text:
DRAM 20%+ hike for next quarter. $SNDK signed LTAs with META. MU locked 16+ LTAs. 800G transceivers revised sharply upward. Lasers sold out through early 2029.

Every demand signal at every supply chain layer says tighten.

Market sells off memory and photonics anyway.

This is a leverage dislocation, not a thesis break. The marginal seller is forced, not informed. Pricing, capacity, and lead times all point the same direction.

The asymmetry is the overshoot.

where's this thesis wrong?

text:
Integrated photonics has delivered groundbreaking advances, but turning a promising prototype into a commercially successful product remains one of the industry's biggest challenges. Many innovations struggle to cross the "valley of death," where manufacturing complexity, yield issues, and process variability prevent laboratory success from scaling to production. 

In this interview, Sreekanth (Sree) Chirayath, Vice President of Business Development at XIVER, discusses why industrialization must be considered from the earliest design stages. He shares practical insights into designing for manufacturability, reducing production risks, and helping integrated photonics technologies transition from research breakthroughs to reliable, scalable products.

Check the link in the comments section for the full interview.

text:
Just in: U.S. semiconductor equipment companies soared 30% before the market opened, with revenue expected to triple in the next fiscal year.

Shares of semiconductor test equipment manufacturer Aehr Test Systems surged about 30% before the U.S. stock market opened on Wednesday, after the company announced strong results, record order data, and gave an astonishing revenue doubling forecast.

As a bullish semiconductor equipment stock based on the AI concept, Aehr's year-to-date growth has reached 256% as of the close of the last trading day, and its stock price has nearly quadrupled in the past year.

(Aehr Test Systems daily chart, source: TradingView) At the financial reporting level, in the fourth quarter ended May 29, the company achieved net revenue of US$18.8 million, higher than US$14.1 million in the same period last year and slightly higher than market expectations of US$18.7 million. Calculated in accordance with U.S.

GAAP, the company's net profit was US$1.4 million, and diluted earnings per share were US$0.04; it recorded a net loss in the same period last year. Non-GAAP net income was $3.6 million, or $0.11 per diluted share, while the market had expected the company to lose $0.01 per share.

(Source: Company official website) The core business of Aehr Test Systems is to conduct high-temperature, high-voltage and long-term power-on testing of the entire wafer before chip packaging. The company's main products are wafer-level/package-level test and burn-in systems, as well as wafer contactors and automatic alignment equipment.

Compared with performance, the company's order performance attracts more attention. According to the financial report, Aehr’s fourth-quarter order volume reached a record high of $60.7 million.

As of May 29, the company's orders on hand were US$80.6 million; if new orders obtained after the end of the financial quarter are included, the "effective unfulfilled orders" reached US$100.6 million. Aehr expects revenue to reach $130 million to $150 million in fiscal 2027, which ends June 25, 2027.

Compared with approximately US$50 million in fiscal year 2026, this means that revenue is expected to increase by up to three times in the next year. The company also expects non-GAAP net profit in fiscal 2027 to be equivalent to 18% to 22% of revenue.

Calculated based on revenue guidance, the corresponding non-GAAP net profit is approximately US$23.4 million to US$33 million. According to Aehr President and CEO Gayn Erickson, the company's future growth momentum will mainly come from four directions. The first is AI wafer-level aging testing.

Leading AI customers are rapidly expanding production and shifting AI accelerator burn-in testing from system level to comprehensive wafer-level testing. The company also completed a benchmark test for a top AI chip supplier, and the results exceeded customer expectations.

The other party plans to promote mass production verification and requires simultaneous evaluation of the second chip. The second is AI chip packaging level testing. A large cloud computing and data center customer continues to place additional orders for Sonoma systems and plans to expand purchases for a second chip with higher power consumption.

The company is also reaching out to more customers for AI accelerators, ASICs, network processors, and edge AI chips for automobiles and robots. The third is silicon photonics.

As AI data centers accelerate the adoption of optical I/O and high-speed interconnects, major customers are expanding production capacity, and another global network equipment giant also expects to continue to add system orders during the year. The company believes silicon photonics has the potential to be a long-term growth engine.

The fourth is silicon carbide and gallium nitride. The silicon carbide market is showing signs of recovery. The company has received approximately US$8 million in new silicon carbide WaferPak orders in the past month. Its customers include leading silicon carbide manufacturers and large global automobile companies.

Vehicle electrification and AI data center power infrastructure are expected to drive demand for SiC and GaN testing back up. In addition, the company is also laying out NAND flash memory and HBM wafer-level testing.

Management believes that Aehr is expected to experience several consecutive years of strong revenue growth as multiple customers enter or expand mass production. (

Shares of semiconductor test equipment manufacturer Aehr Test Systems surged about 30% before the U.S. stock market opened on Wednesday, after the company announced strong results, record order data, and gave an astonishing revenue doubling forecast. As a bullish semiconductor equipment stock based on the AI concept, Aehr's year-to-date growth has reached 256% as of the close of the last trading day, and its stock price has nearly quadrupled in the past year. (Aehr Test Systems daily chart, source: TradingView) At the financial reporting level, in the fourth quarter ended May 29, the company achieved net revenue of US$18.8 million, higher than US$14.1 million in the same period last year and slightly higher than market expectations of US$18.7 million. Calculated in accordance with U.S. GAAP, the company's net profit was US$1.4 million, and diluted earnings per share were US$0.04; it recorded a net loss in the same period last year. Non-GAAP net income was $3.6 million, or $0.11 per diluted share, while the market had expected the company to lose $0.01 per share. (Source: Company official website) The core business of Aehr Test Systems is to conduct high-temperature, high-voltage and long-term power-on testing of the entire wafer before chip packaging. The company's main products are wafer-level/package-level test and burn-in systems, as well as wafer contactors and automatic alignment equipment. Compared with performance, the company's order performance attracts more attention. According to the financial report, Aehr’s fourth-quarter order volume reached a record high of $60.7 million. As of May 29, the company's orders on hand were US$80.6 million; if new orders obtained after the end of the financial quarter are included, the "effective unfulfilled orders" reached US$100.6 million. Aehr expects revenue to reach $130 million to $150 million in fiscal 2027, which ends June 25, 2027. Compared with approximately US$50 million in fiscal year 2026, this means that revenue is expected to increase by up to three times in the next year. The company also expects non-GAAP net profit in fiscal 2027 to be equivalent to 18% to 22% of revenue. Calculated based on revenue guidance, the corresponding non-GAAP net profit is approximately US$23.4 million to US$33 million. According to Aehr President and CEO Gayn Erickson, the company's future growth momentum will mainly come from four directions. The first is AI wafer-level aging testing. Leading AI customers are rapidly expanding production and shifting AI accelerator burn-in testing from system level to comprehensive wafer-level testing. The company also completed a benchmark test for a top AI chip supplier, and the results exceeded customer expectations. The other party plans to promote mass production verification and requires simultaneous evaluation of the second chip. The second is AI chip packaging level testing. A large cloud computing and data center customer continues to place additional orders for Sonoma systems and plans to expand purchases for a second chip with higher power consumption. The company is also reaching out to more customers for AI accelerators, ASICs, network processors, and edge AI chips for automobiles and robots. The third is silicon photonics. As AI data centers accelerate the adoption of optical I/O and high-speed interconnects, major customers are expanding production capacity, and another global network equipment giant also expects to continue to add system orders during the year. The company believes silicon photonics has the potential to be a long-term growth engine. The fourth is silicon carbide and gallium nitride. The silicon carbide market is showing signs of recovery. The company has received approximately US$8 million in new silicon carbide WaferPak orders in the past month. Its customers include leading silicon carbide manufacturers and large global automobile companies. Vehicle electrification and AI data center power infrastructure are expected to drive demand for SiC and GaN testing back up. In addition, the company is also laying out NAND flash memory and HBM wafer-level testing. Management believes that Aehr is expected to experience several consecutive years of strong revenue growth as multiple customers enter or expand mass production. text:
Buzzberg Daily Alpha

We scanned every YouTube video, Substack article, X research post and Reddit discussion in Buzzberg’s latest feed.

MAIN NARRATIVES

Memory is becoming a dispersion trade rather than a single sector bet. Customers are resisting further DRAM price increases, while NAND demand is strengthening as KV-cache offloading allows cheaper flash to replace some DRAM capacity.

CXMT remains the key risk. Its expansion could pressure commodity DRAM and shorten the cycle, but speakers do not yet see it as an immediate competitor in HBM and advanced AI memory, where qualification, yields and packaging remain meaningful barriers.

AI infrastructure is moving from spending plans to production evidence. $ASML is expanding capacity, $AEHR customers are progressing from evaluation toward production, and optical channel checks still indicate constrained photonics and transceiver supply.

Power scarcity also looks underpriced. PJM’s administrative cap suppressed the auction price even as the physical capacity deficit worsened. The lower headline price therefore understates the value of existing dispatchable generation.

WHAT ARE TRACKED SPEAKERS BULLISH ON?

Across all tracked voices, the strongest concentration is in memory and Korea through SK Hynix, Samsung, $MU and $EWY. The thesis combines continued memory tightness with a potential valuation catch-up in Korean equities.

The second cluster is AI infrastructure through $NBIS, $AEHR, $ASML, $AAOI, $NVDA and $SMH. The common view is that demand is progressing from announced capex into production, creating opportunities across AI cloud capacity, burn-in equipment, lithography and optical interconnects.

Financials are also gaining support through $GS, $JPM, $BAC and $MS, driven by recovering capital-markets activity and AI infrastructure financing rather than simple rate-cut exposure.

Among Buzzberg’s Top 50, $NBIS has the clearest convergence, including fresh buying near the close. $AEHR follows as customers move from evaluation into production. SK Hynix has attracted a disclosed dip purchase, while $ASML is being treated as confirmation that semiconductor capacity expansion remains intact.

$AAOI remains a high-conviction hold for one Top 50 voice, but has not yet developed into broader convergence. Overall, the highest-ranked speakers are more concentrated in $NBIS, $AEHR, SK Hynix and $ASML than the broader feed.

text:
$TSEM announced ~$3B expansion in Japan for silicon photonics (SiPho) 300mm, SiGe and advanced packaging

The Japanese government provides ~$1B in subsidies (net ~2B forTSEM)

They raise 2028 guidance: $3B revenue and $1.2B net profit (almost x2 sales and x5 profit vs current)

It is the "floor" of photonic chip manufacturing for CPO key to connect GPUs in AI data centers with less power and more speed

Few fabs do it at 300mm scale

Morgan Stanley already put $TSEM in the CPO supply chain alongside $TSMC and $GFS 

The stock rose ~13% on the day

The demand is real (customers already pay in advance)

Bullish tailwinds for $SIVE

"text": "Just a few days ago Morgan Stanley named the CPO supply chain and put $TSEM in it next to $TSMC and $GFS Tower announced today a roughly $3 billion silicon photonics and silicon germanium capacity expansion in Japan, and the Japanese government is backing about a billion of it in grants, which drops Tower’s net check to around $2 billion. They raised their 2028 model to $3.6 billion in revenue and $1.2 billion in net profit. Against $1.6 billion of trailing revenue and $245 million of net income today, that is roughly double the top line and nearly five times the bottom line, at a 33% net margin. Silicon photonics is the foundry floor of the whole optical buildout, the layer where the photonic chips for co-packaged optics physically get made, and only a handful of merchant fabs on earth can do it at 300mm. This is the type confirmation I want, a supplier on the scarce floor of the optical stack, a government de-risking the capex, and a company guiding to demand real enough to double itself by 2028. You don’t commit three billion dollars unless you know that there is a lot more demand headed you way. text:
Photonic Fabric is the least talked about piece of $MRVL and it targets the single biggest bottleneck in AI: data movement.

Over 60% of AI data center energy is spent just moving data (per imec/Celestial AI analysis), and most of that movement is in the scale-up domain. Hyperscalers buy $40K GPUs that spend much of their time waiting on memory. Fix data movement and you get more usable compute per watt and per dollar from hardware already bought.

1. Breaking the Memory Wall economically

HBM is the scarcest, most contested commodity in AI. Photonic Fabric (PF) attacks its economics directly: a thin HBM3e layer acts as a write-through cache in front of a large, cheap DDR5 pool so the XPU sees terabytes of near HBM speed memory while only a sliver is actual HBM. That's the $/GB reduction.

Today's fixed GPU memory ratio forces the industry to systematically overbuy HBM for worst-case workloads. Pooled, composable memory means capacity flows to wherever the workload is, the same efficiency revolution virtualization brought to CPUs and storage, applied to the most expensive resource in computing.

The enabling piece is OMIB (Optical Multi-chip Interconnect Bridge): a 3D packaging bridge, compatible with Intel EMIB / TSMC CoWoS, that brings the optical connection vertically into the die instead of only at its edges. That frees die-edge beachfront for more HBM and allows 4+ reticle-sized dies in one package, more local HBM and optical access to the pool. Each PF chiplet carries 16 Tbps (Gen1), ~10x today's 1.6T scale-out ports, compact enough to co-package several per XPU.

2. Breaking the Copper Wall

Copper physics caps scale-up domains at roughly one rack (~2-2.5m reach at 200G/lane). PF's Gen2 link targets up to 50m with nanosecond class latency letting thousands of XPUs across multiple racks behave as one coherent "Super XPU" with shared memory. That's exactly what inference-time compute needs: reasoning models demand context/KV-cache capacity and all-to-all bandwidth that no single package can hold.

Platform specs disclosed so far:
• Sub-200ns chip-to-memory, ~100ns NIC-to-NIC — with no retimers needed
• Thermal stability: EAM modulators chosen to survive direct co-packaging against multi-kilowatt XPUs — the reason PF can go deeper into the package than rival photonics
• Memory appliance: 48-72GB HBM3e cache + 2TB DDR5 per module; 16 modules in a 2U chassis = 33TB of unified memory shared by 16 XPUs through a 115 Tbps switch

Gen2 PFLink claims (per $MRVL's own deck): 30x copper's reach, 4x bandwidth, 4x lower power, 4x lower latency at ~1x copper's cost all-in, including laser power. The cost-parity claim is the whole ballgame if it holds; no third-party validation yet.

$AMZN's AWS is the anchor customer (public endorsement + stock warrant), and the earn out structure pays $MRVL team to hit $500M-$2B cumulative revenue by FY2029.

The honest timeline: this is pre-revenue. Per $MRVL's 8-K — meaningful revenue from H2 FY2028, $500M run-rate by Q4 FY2028, $1B by Q4 FY2029. Sampling was targeted for H1 2026 and hasn't been publicly confirmed yet.

Not a faster cable. A different way to build the cluster.

Over 60% of AI data center energy is spent just moving data (per imec/Celestial AI analysis), and most of that movement is in the scale-up domain. https://t.co/4VyTJtyYMA", "id_str": "2078602231793033636", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 1, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "2073186744254828544"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [{"indices": [51, 56], "text": "MRVL"}, {"indices": [2497, 2502], "text": "MRVL"}, {"indices": [2724, 2729], "text": "AMZN"}, {"indices": [2829, 2834], "text": "MRVL"}, {"indices": [2934, 2939], "text": "MRVL"}], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzg2MDIyMzE1MjQ1MTU4NDA=", "text": "Photonic Fabric is the least talked about piece of $MRVL and it targets the single biggest bottleneck in AI: data movement. Over 60% of AI data center energy is spent just moving data (per imec/Celestial AI analysis), and most of that movement is in the scale-up domain. Hyperscalers buy $40K GPUs that spend much of their time waiting on memory. Fix data movement and you get more usable compute per watt and per dollar from hardware already bought. 1. Breaking the Memory Wall economically HBM is the scarcest, most contested commodity in AI. Photonic Fabric (PF) attacks its economics directly: a thin HBM3e layer acts as a write-through cache in front of a large, cheap DDR5 pool so the XPU sees terabytes of near HBM speed memory while only a sliver is actual HBM. That's the $/GB reduction. Today's fixed GPU memory ratio forces the industry to systematically overbuy HBM for worst-case workloads. Pooled, composable memory means capacity flows to wherever the workload is, the same efficiency revolution virtualization brought to CPUs and storage, applied to the most expensive resource in computing. The enabling piece is OMIB (Optical Multi-chip Interconnect Bridge): a 3D packaging bridge, compatible with Intel EMIB / TSMC CoWoS, that brings the optical connection vertically into the die instead of only at its edges. That frees die-edge beachfront for more HBM and allows 4+ reticle-sized dies in one package, more local HBM and optical access to the pool. Each PF chiplet carries 16 Tbps (Gen1), ~10x today's 1.6T scale-out ports, compact enough to co-package several per XPU. 2. Breaking the Copper Wall Copper physics caps scale-up domains at roughly one rack (~2-2.5m reach at 200G/lane). PF's Gen2 link targets up to 50m with nanosecond class latency letting thousands of XPUs across multiple racks behave as one coherent "Super XPU" with shared memory. That's exactly what inference-time compute needs: reasoning models demand context/KV-cache capacity and all-to-all bandwidth that no single package can hold. Platform specs disclosed so far: • Sub-200ns chip-to-memory, ~100ns NIC-to-NIC — with no retimers needed • Thermal stability: EAM modulators chosen to survive direct co-packaging against multi-kilowatt XPUs — the reason PF can go deeper into the package than rival photonics • Memory appliance: 48-72GB HBM3e cache + 2TB DDR5 per module; 16 modules in a 2U chassis = 33TB of unified memory shared by 16 XPUs through a 115 Tbps switch Gen2 PFLink claims (per $MRVL's own deck): 30x copper's reach, 4x bandwidth, 4x lower power, 4x lower latency at ~1x copper's cost all-in, including laser power. The cost-parity claim is the whole ballgame if it holds; no third-party validation yet. $AMZN's AWS is the anchor customer (public endorsement + stock warrant), and the earn out structure pays $MRVL team to hit $500M-$2B cumulative revenue by FY2029. The honest timeline: this is pre-revenue. Per $MRVL's 8-K — meaningful revenue from H2 FY2028, $500M run-rate by Q4 FY2028, $1B by Q4 FY2029. Sampling was targeted for H1 2026 and hasn't been publicly confirmed yet. Not a faster cable. A different way to build the cluster."}}}, "rest_id": "2078602231793033636", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "317", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
🌈 Six worlds. One map. The money is where they touch.

🟡 Photonics: $AAOI $CRDO $SIVEF → light replacing copper, because electrons ran out of headroom
🟢 Compute & neoclouds: $NBIS $IREN $WYFI → the buildout's shovels
🟠 Semis & memory: $MU $SNDK $ADEA $PENG → the substrate everything rides on
🔵 Physical AI: $AMBA $AUR $ONDS → intelligence leaving the screen
🔴 Energy & storage: $TE $FLNC $BE → the buildout's fuel line
🟣 Space: $ASTS $RKLB $PL → the layer above all of it

The insight isn't the circles. It's the seams. Compute × energy decides who scales. Photonics × semis decides who connects. Space × defense decides who gets funded regardless of rates. Single-category companies compete on price. Intersection companies compete on physics. ⚡

Ledger: intersections cut both ways, two worlds de-rating hits twice as hard. The past six weeks proved it.

Own the intersections. That's where pricing power lives. 🌎

text:
$IPGP (IPG Photonics) built its business on fiber lasers that cut and weld metal on factory floors. That business swings with the industrial cycle.

It just agreed to buy Lumibird Medical for €300M in cash: a leader in ophthalmology lasers for cataracts, glaucoma, and macular degeneration. Care people don't skip in a downturn.

Lumibird did €112M of revenue at a 21.5% EBITDA margin last year, and IPG says the deal lifts its gross margin. A metal-cutting laser company buying its way into eyes.

text:
UMC’s order book is full thanks to strong demand from US customers like Qualcomm $QCOM , Intel $INTC , and Sandisk $SNDK .

It is evaluating expansion at its Southern Taiwan Science Park 12AP7 fab to capture more AI-related business in advanced packaging and specialty processes (interposers, DTC, silicon photonics).

PMIC orders are also booming, aligning with tight mature-node supply for AI applications. UMC is deepening ties with US clients, moving from traditional mature processes into wafer-level and advanced packaging integration.

This positions UMC well for sustained AI tailwinds.

#UMC #Semiconductors #AI #AdvancedPackaging

text:
DARPA Perrseus.

https://t.co/mmsyG5SCnr

Thank you @TechInnovationz for bringing this valuable information to my attention. I also check SAM regularly, but honestly, I have no idea how often he checks it. At this point, I am starting to think I simply cannot beat him.

DARPA’s Perrseus solicitation is not simply about building a smaller vacuum chamber. Its broader goal is to miniaturize advanced quantum and atomic sensing technologies and turn them into manufacturable platforms that can eventually be produced at scale.

This is why IonQ’s earlier work on miniaturized XHV technology is particularly relevant. IonQ had already demonstrated a compact, room-temperature XHV prototype by 2025, giving it meaningful experience in one of Perrseus’s most difficult technical areas. That does not mean IonQ has already achieved DARPA’s final requirements, but it does mean the company is starting from a more advanced position than a team beginning from scratch.

The next challenge is manufacturing. Producing a successful prototype is very different from repeatedly manufacturing integrated atomic, MEMS, and photonic devices with consistent performance. This requires foundry capabilities in MEMS, photonics, wafer-level bonding, heterogeneous integration, advanced packaging, process control, and eventually high-volume manufacturing.

That is where the proposed acquisition of SkyWater becomes particularly significant. SkyWater does not already possess a finished Perrseus-class µUHV platform, but once the acquisition is completed, its foundry and process-integration capabilities could provide the manufacturing infrastructure needed to translate IonQ’s vacuum, atomic, and photonic technologies from laboratory prototypes into reproducible, scalable products under one corporate roof.

Perrseus will still require years of research and development, and DARPA has not disclosed the eventual contract values or identified the winning performers. Nor is there any public evidence yet that IonQ will participate.

Nevertheless, the solicitation itself is highly informative. It shows clearly where the U.S. Department of Defense sees strategic value: miniaturized atomic and quantum sensors, advanced MEMS and photonics, and the domestic manufacturing capability required to produce them at scale.

For investors, that may be the more important takeaway. IonQ’s miniaturized XHV technology alone is not the end goal. Its significance lies in how it could combine with IonQ’s broader atomic and photonic capabilities—and potentially SkyWater’s MEMS and foundry infrastructure—to form a vertically integrated manufacturing platform.

In other words, Perrseus does not prove that IonQ will win a DARPA contract. But it does provide another useful reference point showing that the technological direction IonQ has been building toward overlaps remarkably well with areas the U.S. government now considers strategically important.

text:
@damnang2 published a useful framework for thinking about open models and semiconductor demand.

His conclusion is simple: open models do not shrink the hardware market. What gets compressed is the frontier model developer’s premium. The value moves toward hyperscalers, server operators, and the semiconductor infrastructure underneath them.

The data supports that view.

Budget models are taking a larger share of token volume, while Chinese open models have rapidly gained usage. But token demand is growing much faster than per-chip performance. Under the article’s assumptions, that still translates into roughly 2.7× annual growth in chip demand.

Memory may be the even larger bottleneck. MoE reduces the number of parameters used for each token, but it does not eliminate the need to keep the full model available in memory. As resident weights, HBM capacity, and token volume all rise together, the relevant variable becomes the number of bits shipped—not simply the price per bit.

The full Damnang summary and scenario analysis are available to paid subscribers.

I think Kimi K3 makes this framework even more concrete.

K3 is a 2.8-trillion-parameter MoE model with a 1-million-token context window. Only about 50 billion parameters are active per token, but all 2.8 trillion weights must remain available because the router can call different experts at any moment.

The compute is 50B-sized.

The memory footprint is 2.8T-sized.

That means self-hosting demand lands on three layers: memory capacity, memory bandwidth, and the interconnect connecting experts across accelerators.

I have covered those layers in detail:

HBM and optical memory pooling Why memory access—not raw FLOPs—is becoming the serving bottleneck for giant models. https://t.co/tnxgwCtOnd

Coherent DCI Why inference spreading beyond frontier labs increases traffic between datacenters. https://t.co/T9yXrEbmBO

Foundry capacity Why the capacity land grab reached the foundries before the laser shortage became obvious. https://t.co/0lQIcRg4qu

InP light sources Why the more silicon photonics wins, the more InP it ultimately consumes. https://t.co/edvDOfFzVP

Testing and measurement Why some equipment layers benefit regardless of which model, accelerator, or architecture wins. https://t.co/zYvP3vrYgR

Damnang explains why open models do not destroy semiconductor demand.

Kimi K3 shows where that demand is likely to land.

The fiercer the competition becomes at the model layer, the more the hardware layer sells to whoever wins.

"@damnang2 published a useful framework for thinking about open models and semiconductor demand. His conclusion is simple: open models do not shrink the hardware market. What gets compressed is the frontier model developer’s premium. The value moves toward hyperscalers, server operators, and the semiconductor infrastructure underneath them. The data supports that view. Budget models are taking a larger share of token volume, while Chinese open models have rapidly gained usage. But token demand is growing much faster than per-chip performance. Under the article’s assumptions, that still translates into roughly 2.7× annual growth in chip demand. Memory may be the even larger bottleneck. MoE reduces the number of parameters used for each token, but it does not eliminate the need to keep the full model available in memory. As resident weights, HBM capacity, and token volume all rise together, the relevant variable becomes the number of bits shipped—not simply the price per bit. The full Damnang summary and scenario analysis are available to paid subscribers. I think Kimi K3 makes this framework even more concrete. K3 is a 2.8-trillion-parameter MoE model with a 1-million-token context window. Only about 50 billion parameters are active per token, but all 2.8 trillion weights must remain available because the router can call different experts at any moment. The compute is 50B-sized. The memory footprint is 2.8T-sized. That means self-hosting demand lands on three layers: memory capacity, memory bandwidth, and the interconnect connecting experts across accelerators. I have covered those layers in detail: HBM and optical memory pooling Why memory access—not raw FLOPs—is becoming the serving bottleneck for giant models. https://t.co/tnxgwCtOnd Coherent DCI Why inference spreading beyond frontier labs increases traffic between datacenters. https://t.co/T9yXrEbmBO Foundry capacity Why the capacity land grab reached the foundries before the laser shortage became obvious. https://t.co/0lQIcRg4qu InP light sources Why the more silicon photonics wins, the more InP it ultimately consumes. https://t.co/edvDOfFzVP Testing and measurement Why some equipment layers benefit regardless of which model, accelerator, or architecture wins. https://t.co/zYvP3vrYgR Damnang explains why open models do not destroy semiconductor demand. Kimi K3 shows where that demand is likely to land. The fiercer the competition becomes at the model layer, the more the hardware layer sells to whoever wins."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "article": {"article_results": {"result": {"cover_media": {"id": "QXBpTWVkaWE6DAAFCgABHNnMiwVakAAKAAIbd9ik+NqQAwAA", "media_id": "2078917600470536192", "media_info": {"__typename": "ApiImage", "color_info": {"palette": [{"percentage": 89.4, "rgb": {"blue": 247, "green": 249, "red": 250}}, {"percentage": 5.27, "rgb": {"blue": 101, "green": 92, "red": 83}}, {"percentage": 2.24, "rgb": {"blue": 186, "green": 141, "red": 96}}, {"percentage": 0.95, "rgb": {"blue": 131, "green": 183, "red": 223}}, {"percentage": 0.18, "rgb": {"blue": 156, "green": 197, "red": 227}}]}, "original_img_height": 793, "original_img_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HNnMiwVakAA_Qnu.jpg", "original_img_width": 1983}, "media_key": "3_2078917600470536192"}, "id": "QXJ0aWNsZUVudGl0eToyMDc4OTE3NTcwODU4ODI3Nzc2", "lifecycle_state": {"modified_at_secs": 1784487837}, "metadata": {"first_published_at_secs": 1784487837}, "preview_text": "Semiconductor investors have had a run of unsettling news. text:
AI Networking กำลังเข้าสู่รอบลงทุนครั้งใหญ่ หนุนหุ้น Optical และ Silicon Photonics

Mizuho Securities มองว่า การขยายโครงสร้างพื้นฐาน AI ไม่ได้ต้องการเพียง GPU และ HBM แต่ยังผลักดันการลงทุนใน Optical Networking และ Silicon Photonics อย่างมีนัยสำคัญ

🌐 Optical Circuit Switch (OCS) เริ่มได้รับความนิยม

เทคโนโลยี Optical Circuit Switch (OCS) กำลังถูกนำมาใช้งานมากขึ้นในกลุ่ม Hyperscaler

ปัจจุบัน

→ Google $GOOGL เป็นผู้นำการใช้งานผ่านเครือข่าย Jupiter Network

ขณะที่

→ Amazon $AMZN

→ Microsoft $MSFT

อยู่ระหว่างการศึกษาหรือทดลองใช้งาน

ผู้ได้รับประโยชน์สำคัญ ได้แก่

→ Lumentum $LITE

→ Huber+Suhner

🔌 Near-Packaged Optics (NPO) เริ่มมาแรงกว่า CPO

แม้ Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) ยังถูกมองว่าเป็นเทคโนโลยีระยะยาว และอาจเริ่มใช้งานเชิงพาณิชย์ในช่วงปี 2028–2029

แต่ในระยะสั้น อุตสาหกรรมกำลังให้น้ำหนักกับ

Near-Packaged Optics (NPO)

เนื่องจาก

→ ติดตั้งง่ายกว่า

→ ไม่ต้องรวมชิปกับออปติกอย่างซับซ้อน

แม้ประสิทธิภาพด้านพลังงานจะด้อยกว่า CPO เล็กน้อย

💡 Silicon Photonics ยังเผชิญปัญหาอุปทาน

Mizuho ระบุว่า

ความต้องการ Silicon Photonics (SiPho) ยังคงสูงกว่ากำลังการผลิต

โดย Foundry หลักอย่าง

→ GlobalFoundries $GFS

→ TSMC Arizona $TSEM

อาจเผชิญข้อจำกัดด้านกำลังการผลิต

ขณะเดียวกัน ผู้ผลิตกำลังทยอยเปลี่ยนไปใช้ Wafer ขนาด 6 นิ้ว

ซึ่งช่วยเพิ่มพื้นที่ผลิตได้ประมาณ 130%

🚀 Micro-LED อาจเป็นผู้ท้าชิงรายใหม่

Mizuho มองว่า

เทคโนโลยี Micro-LED / ALC

อาจเข้ามาเปลี่ยนตลาด Networking

เนื่องจากสามารถส่งข้อมูลระยะ 20–30 เมตร

ด้วยต้นทุนเพียงประมาณ 1 ใน 5 ของ Optical Pluggable แบบเดิม

และไม่ต้องพึ่งพา Indium Phosphide (InP) ซึ่งกำลังขาดแคลน

บริษัทที่โดดเด่นคือ

→ Credo $CRDO

ซึ่งคาดว่าจะเริ่มส่งมอบผลิตภัณฑ์ในช่วง 2027–2028

📈 Lumentum $LITE

ผู้บริหารระบุว่า

"สิ่งที่ดีที่สุดยังมาไม่ถึง"

เพราะปัจจัยเติบโตสำคัญหลายด้านเพิ่งเริ่มต้น ได้แก่

→ Optical Scale-up

→ Optical Scale-out

→ Optical Circuit Switch

ซึ่งยังไม่ได้สะท้อนเต็มที่ในผลประกอบการ

นอกจากนี้

การเปลี่ยนผ่านสู่ 1.6T Transceiver

นำโดย

→ NVIDIA

→ Google

ยังช่วยผลักดันความต้องการ 200G EML

ซึ่งมีราคาขายเฉลี่ยสูงกว่ารุ่นเดิมประมาณ 2 เท่า

ขณะเดียวกัน

Lumentum ยังร่วมกับ NVIDIA

พัฒนาเลเซอร์กำลังสูงสำหรับ CPO Switch

โดยคาดว่าจะสร้างรายได้เพิ่ม

ประมาณ 50–100 ล้านดอลลาร์

เฉพาะในไตรมาส 4 ปีงบประมาณ 2026

📈 Credo $CRDO

Mizuho ระบุว่า

ในปี 2026

Credo เป็นหนึ่งในหุ้นที่ให้ผลตอบแทนโดดเด่นกว่าคู่แข่งรายใหญ่อย่าง

→ NVIDIA

→ Broadcom

เนื่องจากเชี่ยวชาญด้านการเชื่อมต่อความเร็วสูงที่ประหยัดพลังงาน

หลังเข้าซื้อกิจการ DustPhotonics

Credo มีความแข็งแกร่งด้าน Silicon Photonics มากขึ้น

บริษัทคาดว่า

ธุรกิจ Optical Connectivity

จะสร้างรายได้มากกว่า

600 ล้านดอลลาร์

ในปีงบประมาณ 2027

โดยได้รับแรงหนุนจากการเปลี่ยนผ่านสู่เครือข่าย 1.6T

Securities: Optics Market Trends & Technologies > Optical Circuit Switches (OCS): OCS is gaining traction among hyperscalers, with Google (GOOGL) leading adoption in its Jupiter Network and other hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) exploring its use. Lumentum (LITE) and Huber+Suhner (HUBN) are identified as key partners in this space. > Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) vs. Near-Packaged Optics (NPO): While interest in CPO continues to grow (with potential deliveries in 2028/29E), industry focus is shifting toward NPO in the near-term because it is easier to deploy and requires less co-integration than CPO, despite being less power-efficient. > Silicon Photonics (SiPho) & InP: The supply chain for SiPho remains constrained, with demand potentially outpacing foundry capacities at GFS and TSEM. A transition to 6-inch wafers is underway to help alleviate supply tightness by providing ~130% higher wafer area. > Micro-LED (uLED) Potential: uLED/ALC is emerging as a potential disruptor for networking, offering 20-30m reach at approximately 1/5th the cost of optical pluggables, with no InP supply constraints. Credo (CRDO) is noted as a key player in this space, targeting deliveries starting in 2027-28E. Company-Specific Updates (Lumentum & Credo) Lumentum (LITE): > Growth Drivers: Lumentum management noted that the "best is yet to come" as several major growth drivers—optical scale-up, optical scale-out, and optical circuit switches (OCS)—are just beginning to ramp up and are not yet fully reflected in their numbers. > 1.6T Transition: The company is seeing a significant tailwind from the transition to 1.6T transceivers, led by NVIDIA and Google, with 200-gig EMLs providing roughly double the average selling price (ASP) of previous generations. > Scale-Out Business: The partnership with NVIDIA for high-powered lasers in CPO switch platforms is a major focus, with expected incremental revenue of $50 million to $100 million in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026 alone. Credo Technology (CRDO): > Market Outperformance: Credo has significantly outperformed larger rivals like NVIDIA and Broadcom in share price gains during 2026, largely due to its specialized role in high-speed, energy-efficient connectivity. > Optical Portfolio: Following its acquisition of DustPhotonics, Credo has strengthened its silicon photonics capabilities. The company expects its optical connectivity business to generate over $600 million in revenue in fiscal 2027, supported by a 1.6T networking transition. $LITE $CRDO $AVGO $NVDA $STM $GOOGL $META $TSEM $MRVL $TSM $MSFT $AMZN"}}}, "rest_id": "2078676260998299753", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "12587", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2078954880140771679", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "253", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
this week, 9 members of congress disclosed trades. they sit on armed services, finance, energy & commerce, and homeland security. here's what they bought, what they were voting on, and how long they waited to tell you.

---

sheldon whitehouse. senate finance. environment & public works ranking member. sold $NVDA three separate times across may and june - estimated $400K+ total. simultaneously bought $COHR (AI photonics) and $MU (memory). 156 late filings on record. 811 career trades. rotating out of the famous AI name and into the infrastructure it runs on. filed 13 days late.

---

rick larsen. house transportation & infrastructure ranking member. bought $APH (amphenol - defense/aerospace connectors) and $MCK (mckesson - america's largest drug distributor, still paying opioid settlements) on the same day. sold $ABT and $SPGI the same morning. batch of four. historically averages a 202-day filing delay. 33 late filings on record. this one: 5 days.

---

michael mccaul's spouse. house homeland security vice chair. foreign affairs committee. dropped ~$375,000 into $VTEB (vanguard tax-exempt bond ETF) on june 28. same day: sold $AER (aircraft leasing) for the second time in four days. large defensive rotation by the spouse of the man who oversees border security and foreign threats. filed 14 days after the trade.

---

gilbert cisneros. house armed services. military personnel subcommittee. 14 transactions in a single day on june 29. bought $CDRE (cadre holdings - makes protective gear for law enforcement and military). sold $BRCM. filed on time. the member who votes on military personnel budgets bought the company that outfits them.

---

john fetterman. senate agriculture. food and nutrition subcommittee - the exact committee with jurisdiction over SNAP. sold $LNG (cheniere energy, ~$8K) while the reconciliation bill stripping $187B from food assistance was moving through congress. 15-day delay.

---

thomas kean. energy & commerce. foreign affairs. sold $GOOGL on june 23. filed june 15. 22-day delay. not technically late under the 45-day window. just slow enough to notice. also sold $JNJ (energy & commerce has a health subcommittee) and $SPGI in the same batch.

---

the law requires disclosure within 45 days. the committee assignments are public record. the trades are public record. the connection between what they vote on and what they own is public record.

none of it requires a subpoena. just a EDGAR search and a committee roster.

that's the whole thing.

"media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "this week, 9 members of congress disclosed trades. they sit on armed services, finance, energy & commerce, and homeland security. here's what they bought, what they were voting on, and how long they waited to tell you. --- sheldon whitehouse. senate finance. environment & public works ranking member. sold $NVDA three separate times across may and june - estimated $400K+ total. simultaneously bought $COHR (AI photonics) and $MU (memory). 156 late filings on record. 811 career trades. rotating out of the famous AI name and into the infrastructure it runs on. filed 13 days late. --- rick larsen. house transportation & infrastructure ranking member. bought $APH (amphenol - defense/aerospace connectors) and $MCK (mckesson - america's largest drug distributor, still paying opioid settlements) on the same day. sold $ABT and $SPGI the same morning. batch of four. historically averages a 202-day filing delay. 33 late filings on record. this one: 5 days. --- michael mccaul's spouse. house homeland security vice chair. foreign affairs committee. dropped ~$375,000 into $VTEB (vanguard tax-exempt bond ETF) on june 28. same day: sold $AER (aircraft leasing) for the second time in four days. large defensive rotation by the spouse of the man who oversees border security and foreign threats. filed 14 days after the trade. --- gilbert cisneros. house armed services. military personnel subcommittee. 14 transactions in a single day on june 29. bought $CDRE (cadre holdings - makes protective gear for law enforcement and military). sold $BRCM. filed on time. the member who votes on military personnel budgets bought the company that outfits them. --- john fetterman. senate agriculture. food and nutrition subcommittee - the exact committee with jurisdiction over SNAP. sold $LNG (cheniere energy, ~$8K) while the reconciliation bill stripping $187B from food assistance was moving through congress. 15-day delay. --- thomas kean. energy & commerce. foreign affairs. sold $GOOGL on june 23. filed june 15. 22-day delay. not technically late under the 45-day window. just slow enough to notice. also sold $JNJ (energy & commerce has a health subcommittee) and $SPGI in the same batch. --- the law requires disclosure within 45 days. the committee assignments are public record. the trades are public record. the connection between what they vote on and what they own is public record. none of it requires a subpoena. just a EDGAR search and a committee roster. that's the whole thing."}}}, "rest_id": "2078950794548903989", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "97", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
İnanılmaz 🚨 Bilim insanları imkânsız gibi görünen bir şeyi başardı.

Kuantum teleportasyon gerçek — ama fiziksel maddeyi taşımıyor. Bunun yerine bir parçacığın tam kuantum bilgisini anında başka bir yere gönderiyor ve orada yeniden oluşturuyor.

Orijinal parçacık kaybolurken, kopyası uzakta beliriyor. Sanki gerçekliğin kendisi kopyalanıyor.

Uzmanlar bu keşfin, gelecek nesil iletişim sistemlerinden ultra güçlü kuantum bilgisayarlara kadar dünyayı değiştirecek teknolojilerin kapısını açabileceğini söylüyor.

Kaynak:
Pirandola, S., et al. *Advances in Quantum Teleportation*. Nature Photonics.

text:
If a Chinese company like Moonshot can produce a frontier-level model despite restricted access to Nvidia's most advanced GPUs, investors may ask

Do future AI models really require as many top-end Nvidia chips as previously assumed?

Can software efficiency offset some of the need for massive compute?

If the answer is YES, then the market may lower its expectations for future GPU demand growth. That can hurt Nvidia and companies tied to AI infrastructure (HBM, networking, optics, photonics, testing, etc.).
