text:
$GFS long-term relationship with $SIVE did not begin with the recent AI photonics announcements, it started years earlier with an investment that, in hindsight, illustrates how deep-tech ecosystems are built.

Long before $SIVE acquired MixComm in 2022 for approximately $135 million, $GFS recognized the startup’s potential in millimeter-wave RFIC technology.

Rather than limiting the relationship to that of a manufacturing partner, $GFS took an early minority equity stake in MixComm while closely aligning the company’s designs with its proprietary RF-SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator) process technology.

The move reflected a broader strategy: combining capital investment with manufacturing expertise to help accelerate technologies that could create long-term demand for $GFS specialized semiconductor platforms.

The 2022 acquisition of MixComm by $SIVE marked an important turning point.

Instead of exiting its investment entirely, $GFS chose to convert its ownership in MixComm into publicly traded shares of $SIVE.

That decision distinguished $GFS from purely financial investors.

By maintaining an equity position beyond the transaction and after the lock-up period, the company signaled confidence not only in $SIVE immediate 5G opportunities but also in the broader technology roadmap the combined business could pursue.

Looking back from 2026, that strategy appears increasingly significant.

Technologies originally developed to address high-frequency wireless communications are now finding applications well beyond the 5G market.

As artificial intelligence drives unprecedented demand for faster and more energy-efficient data center infrastructure, photonic interconnects have emerged as one of the industry's most important growth areas.

Against this backdrop, the AI photonics collaboration announced by $GFS and $SIVE in June 2026 represents more than a new commercial partnership.

It reflects years of strategic alignment built through technology development, manufacturing collaboration, and equity ownership.

By combining $SIVE advanced laser technology with $GFS high-volume silicon photonics manufacturing platform, the companies are targeting one of the key challenges facing next-generation AI data centers: enabling scalable, high-bandwidth optical connectivity while improving power efficiency.

In an industry where partnerships often evolve as quickly as technology itself, sustained equity ownership can provide valuable insight into long-term strategic priorities.

$GFS continued position as a shareholder suggests that its original investment in MixComm was never solely about participating in the 5G cycle.

Instead, it formed part of a broader vision, one that has evolved from millimeter-wave communications to the rapidly expanding AI photonics ecosystem now shaping the future of hyperscale computing.

Long before $SIVE acquired MixComm in 2022 for approximately $135 million, $GFS recognized the startup’s potential in millimeter-wave RFIC technology. Rather than limiting the relationship to that of a manufacturing partner, $GFS took an early minority equity stake in MixComm while closely aligning the company’s designs with its proprietary RF-SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator) process technology. The move reflected a broader strategy: combining capital investment with manufacturing expertise to help accelerate technologies that could create long-term demand for $GFS specialized semiconductor platforms. The 2022 acquisition of MixComm by $SIVE marked an important turning point. Instead of exiting its investment entirely, $GFS chose to convert its ownership in MixComm into publicly traded shares of $SIVE. That decision distinguished $GFS from purely financial investors. By maintaining an equity position beyond the transaction and after the lock-up period, the company signaled confidence not only in $SIVE immediate 5G opportunities but also in the broader technology roadmap the combined business could pursue. Looking back from 2026, that strategy appears increasingly significant. Technologies originally developed to address high-frequency wireless communications are now finding applications well beyond the 5G market. As artificial intelligence drives unprecedented demand for faster and more energy-efficient data center infrastructure, photonic interconnects have emerged as one of the industry's most important growth areas. Against this backdrop, the AI photonics collaboration announced by $GFS and $SIVE in June 2026 represents more than a new commercial partnership. It reflects years of strategic alignment built through technology development, manufacturing collaboration, and equity ownership. By combining $SIVE advanced laser technology with $GFS high-volume silicon photonics manufacturing platform, the companies are targeting one of the key challenges facing next-generation AI data centers: enabling scalable, high-bandwidth optical connectivity while improving power efficiency. In an industry where partnerships often evolve as quickly as technology itself, sustained equity ownership can provide valuable insight into long-term strategic priorities. $GFS continued position as a shareholder suggests that its original investment in MixComm was never solely about participating in the 5G cycle. Instead, it formed part of a broader vision, one that has evolved from millimeter-wave communications to the rapidly expanding AI photonics ecosystem now shaping the future of hyperscale computing."}}}, "rest_id": "2078865496242586009", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5181", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

It's so much BS

text:
AI is no longer one trade.

It’s compute, memory, and interconnect.
That’s why I keep circling $MU, $MRVL, $CRDO, $ALAB, $AAOI and $AMAT.

Analyst reporting says AI optics are shifting toward LPO, CPO and OCS as copper hits its limits. Reuters is also reporting Iran related supply chain stress with PCB prices up as much as 40% and copper foil up as much as 30% this year.

The market still wants to pretend this is only about GPUs. It isn’t.

The real money is in the bottlenecks.
$QQQ $SPY #AI #semis #photonics #stocks

text:
$CRDO 
クレドの最大のチャンスは依然として過小評価されています
Jul 19, 2026, 10:02 AM ET
Pythia Research
概要
最近のアナリストによる325ドルから350ドルへの引き上げは、株価30%の調整にもかかわらず、Credoがより広範なAI接続プラットフォームへと移行していることを裏付けています。

より遅いコパッケージ光学機器の採用はAECの滑走路を延ばし、CPOは2026年に浸透率が0.5%にとどまると予測されています。

光ネットワーク市場は2031年までに230億ドルから1120億ドルへと拡大し、複数の成長サイクルを支える見込みです。

コンセンサスでは、2031会計年度までに収益が24.5億ドルから67.8億ドルに増加すると予想されており、現在のプレミアム評価はますます妥当に見えるように見えます。
------------------------------------------------------------
1. 株価30%下落でも事業の強さはむしろ確認された
6月高値から30%以上下落したが、事業悪化の兆候はなし。

下落要因は「マクロ環境・利益確定」であり、ファンダメンタルはむしろ強化。

2. アナリスト評価が一斉に上方修正
Evercore：$325

Stifel：$350

Bank of America：$340   → いずれも現在のコンセンサスを大きく上回る。 → 理由は「Credo が AEC企業ではなく AI接続プラットフォームへ進化している」点。
3. CPO（コパッケージ光学）の普及遅れはむしろ追い風
2026年のCPO浸透率は 0.5% に留まる見通し。

Copper（AEC）需要が長期化し、Credoの既存製品の収益機会が拡大。
Jensen Huang も「次世代AIシステムは copper と optics の両方が必要」と明言。

4. 光ネットワーク市場は 2031年までに 23B → 112B へ拡大

CAGR 30% の巨大成長市場。

800G / 1.6T、シリコンフォトニクス、CPOなど複数の成長サイクルが連続する。
5. 同業他社も高成長 → 構造的なインフラ投資サイクル

Astera Labs：+93%

Lumentum：+90%   → 単なる在庫積み上げではなく、本物のAIインフラ投資サイクル。
6. 高いバリュエーションは「割高ではなく妥当」

EV/Sales：15.2x
P/E：33.9x

EV/EBITDA：28.7x → 一見高いが、
売上は FY2027：2.45B → FY2031：6.78B（+177%）
PEG：0.75x（Broadcomと同水準）
Astera Labsよりはむしろ割安 → 複数の成長サイクルを持つ企業として妥当。
7. 強気シナリオの鍵は「プラットフォーム化」

Copper / Optics / Silicon Photonics / Software / Memory Connectivity → これらを単一製品ではなくスタックとして採用できるかが重要。

リスク：
顧客集中（Hyperscaler依存）
光学製品の立ち上がり遅延
高バリュエーションゆえの実行リスク
8. 結論

Credoは「光学企業」ではなく、 AIインフラ全体を貫く接続プラットフォーム企業へ進化中。 この構造的な変化は市場にまだ十分織り込まれておらず、 長期的なアップサイド余地は大きいという評価。

text:
AI's next optical bottleneck may be the laser

Citi estimates that data-center demand for continuous-wave laser chips will reach about 223 million units in 2026 against supply of roughly 198 million. By 2028, demand approaches 990 million while supply reaches about 638 million. Under that model, demand would exceed supply by 55%.

■ Bandwidth upgrades move the bottleneck into light sources

As AI clusters shift from 800G to 1.6T links, switches and GPUs require more high-speed optical connectivity. Silicon photonics can integrate modulation and transmission, but it still needs reliable indium-phosphide lasers as the light source. Wider adoption of co-packaged and near-packaged optics increases the number of sources required across the system.

Supply is expanding. Coherent has moved a six-inch indium-phosphide platform into volume production, allowing more chips per wafer, while other vendors are adding capacity. Yield, packaging, and customer qualification can still limit the ramp even after factory space is added.

This is a demand scenario, not a shortage already guaranteed to happen. Shipments of 800G and 1.6T equipment, six-inch wafer yields, laser pricing, and lead times will test the forecast. A slower demand ramp or faster yield improvement would rewrite the 55% gap.

Source: Citi Research and Coherent OFC 2026 Investor Event (Image: @pequityresearch)

text:
The recent selloff in AI/photonics looks dramatic on the chart, but I think the market is confusing positioning with fundamentals. 

Since early July, $LITE has corrected roughly 33% from its all-time high, while $COHR $AAOI and $CRDO have all experienced double-digit declines despite no material deterioration in customer demand, order activity or forward guidance. That's a classic sign of multiple compression rather than a broken investment thesis.

This is also not happening in isolation. Momentum funds are unwinding crowded AI positions, ETFs have been rebalancing following Russell reconstitution, macro investors are rotating around Fed expectations and geopolitical risks, while short sellers have become increasingly vocal after one of the strongest AI rallies in history. $SPY $QQQ $VIXY $SOXX 

these flows can overwhelm fundamentals for weeks..

Meanwhile, the data that actually matters continues pointing in the opposite direction. Hyperscalers are expected to spend more than $700 billion on AI infrastructure this year, $TSM still says AI demand exceeds supply, $NVDA continues expanding networking capacity and optical content per AI cluster keeps increasing as GPU deployments scale toward hundreds of thousands of accelerators.

I think the market is once again pricing the next headline instead of the next earnings report. I've seen this movie before with DeepSeek, valuation scares, recession fears etc..

Different narrative, same outcome. Until AI capex, customer orders and earnings start rolling over, I see this as another healthy reset inside a secular AI infrastructure supercycle, not the end of it.

Since early July, $LITE has corrected roughly 33% from its all-time high, while $COHR $AAOI and $CRDO have all experienced double-digit declines despite no material deterioration in customer demand, order activity or forward guidance. That's a classic sign of multiple compression rather than a broken investment thesis. This is also not happening in isolation. Momentum funds are unwinding crowded AI positions, ETFs have been rebalancing following Russell reconstitution, macro investors are rotating around Fed expectations and geopolitical risks, while short sellers have become increasingly vocal after one of the strongest AI rallies in history. $SPY $QQQ $VIXY $SOXX these flows can overwhelm fundamentals for weeks.. Meanwhile, the data that actually matters continues pointing in the opposite direction. Hyperscalers are expected to spend more than $700 billion on AI infrastructure this year, $TSM still says AI demand exceeds supply, $NVDA continues expanding networking capacity and optical content per AI cluster keeps increasing as GPU deployments scale toward hundreds of thousands of accelerators. I think the market is once again pricing the next headline instead of the next earnings report. I've seen this movie before with DeepSeek, valuation scares, recession fears etc.. Different narrative, same outcome. Until AI capex, customer orders and earnings start rolling over, I see this as another healthy reset inside a secular AI infrastructure supercycle, not the end of it."}}}, "rest_id": "2078830716486578329", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "9171", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
🌍 The infrastructure the next decade runs on. Eight lanes, one map. 🧵👇

💡 Photonics → light replaces copper. The interconnect is the new bottleneck.
$CRDO $AAOI 
⚙️ AI infrastructure → the compute buildout, still early on megawatts actually energized.
$NBIS $IREN $WYFI 
🤖 Physical AI → intelligence that acts. The models are leaving the chat window.
$OUST $AMBA 
🛸 Autonomy & drones → machines that operate themselves, and the defense budgets that want them.
$ONDS $MRLN 
⚡ Energy & storage → the buildout's binding constraint. No electrons, no tokens.
$TE $EOSE $QS 
🪨 Critical minerals → the hard constraints under everything. Geology doesn't do earnings guidance.
$MP $ASPI $UAMY 
🚀 Space → the orbital economy: coverage, imaging, launch. The high ground, literally.
$ASTS $RKLB $PL 

The thesis in one line: own the chokepoints the digital economy can't function without. Logos change. Layers don't. 🔑

The ledger: infrastructure de-rates violently when positioning crowds, and the past six weeks were the live demonstration. Chokepoints don't exempt you from drawdowns. They exempt you from irrelevance.

Own the layers, not the logo. 

DYOR. Not FA.

text:
I have no disagreements here with @michaelsikand 

One additional very important Caveat I would like to add is, multi decade AI buildout doesn’t mean winners are obvious today. 

Heck for all we know the real winners aren’t even visible yet. Thematic success and a company success are two very different things. 

$SIVE or $AAOI or $CRDO or $COHR might be the poster child for photonics today but for all we know right now, they might not be there to see photonics win. Execution is of paramount importance and surprisingly few are actually good at it. 

All I can tell is keep watching the moves, follow the earnings, keep questioning the thesis and never borrow conviction. 

I was a bit surprised with X world reaction to @bubbleboi because he chose to do this and change based on new info according to him! That’s normal and cannot be something to be ridiculed..! 

What names do you think can execute well in this AI buildout? 

Bookmark this so that you can come back to see who got it right.

We are in the middle of a multi-decade buildout.

Yes, decades. Across AI, space, defense etc.

This journey is not going to be a straight path up and to the right.

As painful as they are, corrections are critical to ensure we are not blindly floating up in a bubble.

text:
Interesting institutional validation for the $SIVE thesis.

A Morgan Stanley report on co-packaged optics names Sivers alongside Lumentum and Coherent as a key laser supplier, while highlighting that CPO could reduce optical-interconnect power by roughly 50–80%.

The report argues that value will increasingly shift toward suppliers controlling optical engines, silicon photonics, external lasers and advanced packaging.

That is exactly where the Sivers opportunity sits. Silicon photonics still requires an external light source, making qualified InP laser suppliers strategically important as CPO scales.

This is not a revenue confirmation, and Sivers still has to prove high-volume execution. But being included in the same institutional CPO supply-chain map as $NVDA, $AVGO, Ayar Labs, $TSM, $GFS, $LITE and $COHR is meaningful recognition for a company of its size.

A ~$1.5B company named by Morgan Stanley alongside the two dominant Western laser players. Promising if i have to say so.

text:
This is the CPO supply chain point people keep underestimating.

Everyone talks about switches and optical engines, but the laser layer is one of the real chokepoints.

If Morgan Stanley is highlighting $SIVE, $COHR, and $LITE as key laser suppliers and China is still behind on CPO-grade lasers then the Western laser stack becomes strategically important.

That makes $SIVE interesting.

Small-cap market value, but sitting next to $COHR and $LITE in a supply chain that could become foundational for scale-up AI networking.

CPO may take time to ramp, but when it does, the bottlenecks won’t just be Nvidia/Broadcom switches or $TSM photonics capacity.

They’ll be the specialized component suppliers that make the architecture actually work.

Lasers are not the loudest part of the CPO story.
They may be one of the most important.

text:
Morgan Stanley’s latest CPO (Co-Packaged Optics) report is timely and reinforces my core thesis on the optical interconnect supply chain.

CPO is emerging as the clear path forward for next-gen high-performance AI interconnects, with the laser source remaining the most critical bottleneck today.

The CPO theme is still in the early validation stage, creating alpha opportunities across lasers, optical engines, and silicon photonics foundry plays. 

Small-cap “high-quality compounders” like $SIVE offer the highest upside elasticity — but also the highest volatility.

AI optical interconnects are accelerating. 

$NVDA $AVGO $TSM $SIVE

text:
Morgan Stanley note on CPO today. Key participants include:

- $SIVE, $COHR, and $LITE in laser supply
- Broadcom and Nvidia in switch platforms
- Lightmatter, Ayar Labs, Marvell/Celestial, and POET in optical engines and photonics, 
- $TSM, $GFS, and $TSEM in silicon photonics foundry capacity

I’ve covered all of these before, like Tower Semi. 

But I’m especially happy that Morgan Stanley validated my research that $SIVE is one of the critical global players in CPO.

A small $1.5B laser company next to your two leading $60B+ companies…

If you also synthesize Rosenblatt Securities recent note that China laser suppliers were quite far from having CPO lasers.

This kinda magnifies importance of the three Western leaders of that laser chokepoint.

text:
Over the years, co-packaged optics (#CPO) has been demonstrated with a variety of 💥light-source architectures, showing that there is no single universal approach. Some notable examples include:
✮IBM Power 775 (2010): one of the first commercial CPO implementations, using 850 nm VCSEL arrays
✮Hewlett Packard Enterprise’s 2 × 12.8 Tb/s switch demo (2012): also based on VCSELs but different wavelength than IBM (990nm?).
✮Rockley Photonics, Broadcom, Cisco, and Ayar Labs: using remote / external CW lasers
✮Intel: demonstrating integrated-laser with silicon photonics
✮RANOVUS Inc.: using a quantum-dot multi-wavelength laser platform, with both integrated and external-source variants
✮Avicena Tech LightBundle: using micro-LEDs, which are also relevant for CPO-style interconnect applications
Each architecture brings different trade-offs in energy efficiency, reach, latency, cost per bit, and technology readiness.
The table below summarizes the key attributes of the main light-source choices for CPO. It seems there is no one-size-fits-all answer. The right choice depends on reach, power budget, cost target, packaging approach, and manufacturing maturity.
P.S: Is my collection of CPO implementations complete? Productive feedback is welcome❤️

text:
The semicap/photonics/memory bros collectively don’t seem to think technicals matter

Most even outwardly saying TA is for clowns

If you have no risk management and don’t re about losses then yeah, things can stay overheated longer than they can stay cooled

Key word is risk management

Serenity is still up 15X this year after a 50% drop, but I’d bet anyone who was a follower without critical thinking or risk management, is down big on the year

What you buy + when you buy it, it’s a combo

Nothing in a vacuum

text:
BLUF: What is $LASE ?

$LASE (Laser Photonics) develops industrial laser systems used for cleaning, cutting, marking, engraving, and surface preparation. Instead of chemicals or abrasive blasting, its technology uses high-powered lasers to remove rust, paint, coatings, and contaminants—serving markets like defense, aerospace, manufacturing, energy, and infrastructure.

Bull Case: If laser cleaning continues replacing traditional methods, $LASE could benefit from a multi-year shift toward safer, faster, and more environmentally friendly industrial maintenance. Growth in defense spending, reshoring of manufacturing, and aging infrastructure all create potential demand. Long term, success depends on scaling revenue, expanding its product lineup, and proving its technology can win recurring commercial and government contracts.

Bear Case: $LASE is still a very small company with limited revenue and faces execution risk. The industrial laser market is competitive, larger players have significantly more resources, and the company must consistently convert interest into meaningful sales. Until revenue growth and profitability become more predictable, the stock will likely remain highly volatile.

Long term, this is an execution story—not a hype story. If management can consistently grow sales, secure larger contracts, and establish Laser Photonics as a leader in industrial laser cleaning, today’s valuation could look inexpensive. If execution stalls, however, the upside thesis becomes much harder to justify.

text:
$SIVE is the laser inside Ayar Labs' SuperNova light source. Not a guess. Confirmed directly by both companies three separate times.

ECOC 2022. Sivers Photonics and Ayar Labs jointly demonstrated an 8-wavelength DFB laser array powering SuperNova.

OFC 2023. Same demonstration, expanded, still explicitly branded as Sivers Photonics powering Ayar Labs' SuperNova.

ECOC 2024. Sivers' 16-wavelength laser array integrated into SuperNova's second generation, with Ayar Labs' own CTO Vladimir Stojanovic quoted directly calling Sivers a key partner in bringing this technology to market at scale.

So when bears say $SIVE has no real connections, I'm not sure how much more confirmed you can get than this. Meanwhile Ayar Labs is inside Nvidia's own NVLink Fusion ecosystem now, backed by Nvidia as an investor, and $SIVE is the only laser supplier listed by name on their website.

Ayar Labs’ CPO solution: https://t.co/rAPJvMKwqe

text:
During this "everything" crash, I'm focused on strong AI themes: memory, neoclouds, photonics, and robotics.

Neocloud stocks like $WYFI, $WULF, $NBIS, $IREN, $CRWV, $CIFR, etc. may continue to experience turbulence if $WGMI can't close above weekly gold.

Positioned for any scenario.

If it holds, then we're good to go.

If it doesn't hold and it's time to derisk, then we can start getting excited about accumulating our favorite names at increasingly better prices.

I completely agree... the volatility in high beta names can be scary, especially when a big stock market index like the KOSPI is down just as much!

But I'll never stay quiet on a red day.

Neocloud fundamentals remain rock solid... with constant tailwinds like the recent $CLSK deal and many others.

Things may look bleak now but the future has never been brighter IMO.

Just gotta focus on survival. 🙏

Will highlight entries as they come.

text:
Did You Know?

The biggest challenge facing AI isn't building faster processors. It's moving information between them.

As AI clusters scale, traditional copper interconnects are increasingly constrained by bandwidth, power consumption, and thermal performance. Conventional optical links extend reach but add complexity, higher power demands, and greater cost.

That's why Kopin and FabricAI are developing Neural I/o™, a MicroLED-based optical interconnect technology designed to adress the bandwidth, latency, and power challenges shaping the next generation of AI infrastructure. 

Learn more: https://t.co/PPueVYwPfS

#KopinTech #ArtificialIntelligence #AIInfrastructure #Photonics #DataCenters #AI #Neuralio #MicroLED @FabricAIFABC

text:
In my eyes, the selloff in high-beta stocks is mostly sentiment driven, and not driven by fundamental changes.

We have seen some FUD lines in the past few weeks:
> $META announcing they will rent out excess capacity compute. Panic across the board because people think they will reduce CAPEX. But it is quite the opposite actually according to the latest news.

> CXMT IPO and the fear that CXMT and YMTC will flood the Western market with cheap memory. Complete utter nonsense, they can barely supply the domestic market and Western hyperscalers and CSPs are not going to use Chinese memory.

> SemiAnalysis CPO report was very sus. The fact that they published a bearish report and formed a photonics ETF after the sector dropped 40-50% makes me wary.

In my eyes, the selloff in the AI infrastructure sector with $AAOI, $SNDK, $MU, $SKHY, $KXIAY, you name it, is mostly driven by sentiment, not by fundamentals.

Sentiment may dictate the price action in the short-term, in the longer term fundamentals will prevail.

text:
Join us at ADLM 2026 as the global laboratory medicine and point-of-care community comes together.  

Hamamatsu will exhibit an assortment of high performance, miniature photonic solutions designed to bring laboratory testing to the point-of-care market. Hamamatsu will show state-of-the-art sensor modules based on photon counting and mini-spectrometers that provide ultimate sensitivity along with industry leading reliability, a combination critical in healthcare diagnostic systems.  

Connect with the Hamamatsu team at ADLM and explore what’s next in diagnostic innovation. Learn more about the event: https://t.co/5Odd8Dag6q  

Book an appointment with our experts: https://t.co/Fq6AIdSWth

#ADLM2026 #Photonics #LifeSciences #PointofCare #OpticsEngineering
